in your News Feed. If you find that a person, group or Page consistently posts things you don�t wantto see, you may want to limit how often you see their posts or remove them from your Facebookexperience.
We know these options may not apply to every situation, so please let us know if you see something
else you think we should take a look at. You can also learn more about our specific standards onnudity and the kinds of images allowed on Facebook.

AGREED ORDER FOR MODIFICATION 
THIS CAUSE having come before the Court on the Motion To Modify Prior 
Order Of The Court and the Court having been advised that the Parties have reached an 
agreement in this cause and the Court finding that said agreement is in the best interest of 
the child of the parties and therefore the Court adopts the Agreed Order as order of the 
Court and therefore orders as follows: 
IT IS THEREFORE ORDERED AND ADJUDGED that there has been a 
material change in circumstances in this cause and accordingly the Petitioner, Matthew 
Reardon, is ordered and allowed visitation with the minor child of the parties, namely, 
Lydia Elise Reardon, as follows: 
l. Beginning January 1, 2018, Matthew Reardon shall have visitation with the said 
minor child every other week-end from 5:00 p.m. Friday afternoon until 5:00 p.m. 
Sunday afternoon at his residence in Water Valley, Mississippi. 
2. The Parties, Matthew Reardon and Phyllis Crowder, mutually agree to meet at the 
Water Valley Police Department. or some other mutually agreed upon location 
outside of Lafayette County, Mississippi, for exchange of the child for visitation 
purposes. 
A. Summer 
Matthew Reardon shall have visitation with the minor child for (4) four weeks 
during the summer to be exercised as agreed upon by Parties.

wherever he may be found.

THE COURT : Sheriff  East, you've already  given  the oath, so you're still under  that oath. Deputies , if y'all

CASE  REGARDING  INCIDENT  AT SHERIFF  S DEPARTMENT  PARKING  LOT
 (December  28, 2020 )
PROSECUTOR : I'm going  to call Deputy  Dixon . That's the rule being  voted . I see you.
THE COURT : You may.
DEPUTY  COURTNEY  DIXON  - DIRECT  EXAMINATION
PROSECUTOR : Courtney  Dixon  of the Lafayette  County  Sheriff 's Department . Is that correct , sir?
DEPUTY  DIXON : Yes, sir.
PROSECUTOR : I direct  your attention  to the 28th day of December  2020  and your encounter  of Matthew  
Reardon  on that day. First of all, is Matthew  Reardon  the person  that you encountered  that day here with 
us in the courtroom ?
DEPUTY  DIXON : Yes, sir.
PROSECUTOR : What  was your purpose ? Tell us about  that encounter . What  was going  on? Where  were  
you? And what  was happening ?
DEPUTY  DIXON : I went  inside  the break  room .
PROSECUTOR : Tell me where  you are when  you say break  room .
DEPUTY  DIXON : We're all inside  the Sheriff 's Department . And I walked  out of that hall.
PROSECUTOR : I know  this is going  to sound  a little bit silly, but this is the Lafayette  County  Sheriff 's 
Department  inside  of Lafayette  County . Go for it. You were  in the break  room  inside  the department .
DEPUTY  DIXON : When  I looked  outside  in our designated  parking  areas , I seen  a black  Kia Soul. It's a 
2013  Kia Soul, black  in color . It was parked  in the Sheriff 's Department 's designated  areas  where  deputies  
park at.
\n--- Page 2 ---
PROSECUTOR: So when you say it's labeled off, like if you look out of the break room, you can see the 
parking lot, through glass windows, and it's right there at the front. When you say, how would an 
individual know not to park in those spots?
DEPUTY DIXON: We have signs that say, "Reserved for Sheriff's Department." We have signs right there 
on the side.
PROSECUTOR: Of course working Sheriff's Department y'all are there somebody's probably there 24-7 or 
have access for that and they need to get in there in a hurry if they need to correct?
DEPUTY DIXON: Correct.
PROSECUTOR: So you see a vehicle in that spot. Describe how the vehicle is parked.
DEPUTY DIXON: The Black Kia Soul was backed into the area, into that parking, that is in that area, and it 
was taking up two parking spots. It was parked over the line. So I walked outside and another deputy 
sheriff's, it was a Durango, pulled up. It was Ethan Tipple. He was walking up towards me as I was walking 
down a little walkway where we've got gang access to. When Ethan Tipple passed the Black Kia Soul, Matt 
Reardon, I said, "Matt, you cannot park there."
PROSECUTOR: Well, when you went out there, did you know there was anybody in the vehicle?
DEPUTY DIXON: At the time, no.
PROSECUTOR: You went out there because you saw a vehicle in the area. Was somebody actually in 
there?
DEPUTY DIXON: Yes.
PROSECUTOR: So, Mr. Reardon, do you have identified or he exited out of the vehicle?
DEPUTY DIXON: He exited out of the vehicle. I said, "Matt, you cannot park there." He says, "I will not 
move until I speak to the sheriff." I said, "Matt, you cannot park there." He said, once again. I said, "I'm 
going to tell you one last time. Move the vehicle. This is a reserved area for the sheriff's department." He 
said, "I will not move, I need to speak to the sheriff, Joey East." I said, "Put your hands behind your back. 
You're going to be under arrest for disorderly conduct." He began to run away, chanting that he needs to 
speak to the sheriff or things of that nature and he was screaming, screaming it loudly as I gave chase 
behind him. Deputy Ethan Tipple gave chase also. He's running sideways so it wasn't like a long chase but 
it was short and I restrained him, took him down, and as we was trying to place him in cuffs he was in like 
a fetal position down on his knees and so he couldn't tell who got on his legs to hold his legs because he 
was squirming. While I tried to get his hands from under him, from under his chest because he was tight. 
He had his arms like he was very tense. So as I was reaching under him, I scraped over my knuckles. I had 
scratches on my knuckles trying to get from under him from the pavement. We finally got him in cuffs. I 
\n--- Page 3 ---
had to remove his cell phone and things up in his hand. I don't know if he had two or he may have had 
two cell phones. I had to remove them from his hands before placing them in cuffs. I kind of threw them 
to the side. He was cursing and saying all kinds of things while we got him in cuffs and everything. 
Walked him inside the jail. He began to kick the doors and everything. Continued to curse. And his 
attitude was so bad that we had to put him in a restraining chair.
PROSECUTOR: All this for parking in the reserved area for the sheriff's department?
DEPUTY DIXON: For two reserved areas for the Sheriff's Department?
PROSECUTOR: Yes. One and a portion of the... What else happened? Was that that?
DEPUTY DIXON: Well, basically just cursing and banging inside the jail. I know that he did what I told 
him, my chairman just told him. A few photos to keep long of that. That I think I can resort back to him in 
the morning and see what all happened. But as far as I know, I know that he did, let's see, when we made

DEFENSE COUNSEL: Okay. And then the violence, was that what occurred while you were trying to take 
him down?
DEPUTY DIXON: Yes.
DEFENSE COUNSEL: Okay. And as I understand it, he kind of balled up.
DEPUTY DIXON: Yeah, he was doing some movement with his feet. It looked as if he was kicking from 
my point of view.
DEFENSE COUNSEL: Alright, you were focused on getting control of him.
DEPUTY DIXON: Yes.
DEFENSE COUNSEL: Certainly not looking at, studying what his feet were doing the whole time, right?
DEPUTY DIXON: Yes.
DEFENSE COUNSEL: And did he have some electronics in his hand?
DEPUTY DIXON: He did. I'm not sure what he was doing with it, but I'm pretty sure he was recording. He 
had, I seen myself with, I want to say he had a cord on it, probably some earbuds or whatever. And once I 
got that out of his hand, we were...
DEFENSE COUNSEL: And when he was searched, did he have a recording device in his front pocket?
DEPUTY DIXON: He had something. I'm not sure.
DEFENSE COUNSEL: Okay. So he could have been protecting those devices as he goes down?
DEPUTY DIXON: Could have been. Not sure.
\n--- Page 13 ---
DEFENSE COUNSEL: Or turning them on or off?
DEPUTY DIXON: I'm not sure. What it looked like for me, he wasn't trying to get put in cuffs because he 
kept saying no. He was moving. He did say, "I was moving my vehicle, I was moving my vehicle," but if 
you're moving your vehicle, why would you run away?
DEFENSE COUNSEL: Gotcha. You're bigger than he is, aren't you?
DEPUTY DIXON: I am.
DEFENSE COUNSEL: He never swung at you, though, did he? He never really tried to injure you?
DEPUTY DIXON: Not at all. I don't know.

DEPUTY DIXON: No.

then you just drop the ball?

IN THE JUSTICE COURT OF LAFAYETTE COUNTY, MISSISSIPPI 
STATE OF MISSISSIPPI  
VS. CAUSE NOS.  9290609 , 9290610 , 
9291197 , 9291198 , 9291202  
MATTHEW REARDON  
 
 
MOTION TO ALTER OR AMEND JUDGEMENT UNDER M.R.C.P 59(E)  
OR IN THE  ALTERNATIVE TO VACATE JUDGEMENT ENTERED PURSUANT TO M.R.C.P 
60(B)(1,2 ,4,6) 
 
 
Comes now before the court Matthew Reardon, your Defendant, whom respectfully moves t his 
court under the above -mentioned cause numbers to grant Defendant�s MOTION TO ALTER OR 
AMEND JUDGEMENT or in the alternative TO VACATE THE JUDGEMENT ENTERED. On 
Multipl e Occasions prior to trial commencing, Defendant made the State known of major 
deficiencies  which  were  inconsistent with Due Process  of Law,  and in fact the violations 
amounted to a complete deprivation of Defendant�s Due Process Rights Defendant of fers th e 
following justifying facts and reasons for this request to be GRANTED in support o f his motion:  
 
I. The Introduction and Use of Knowingly Perjurious Testimony in Order to Procure a Con viction 
 
 
1. In the police reports of Dixon and Tidwell, both deputies made distinct reference  to 
Defendant verbalizing �No� three separate times . However, Defendant never once said No 
as the deputies claimed in their statements, and as such Defendant has alleged that t his   
dangerous discovery  would raise much more than a suspicion that these two  deputies 
conspired together to falsely justify their very physical arrest that neve r should have been 
made, and in doing so certainly demonstrates a conspiracy  between two o fficers on behalf 
of the state acting under color of law to deprive a private citizen of his civil/consti tutional 
rights which  he was trying to protect when he commuted to the Lafayette County Sheriffs 
Office on 12/28/2020 in the first place. 
\n--- Page 2 ---
2. Dixon and Tidwell both stated that Defendant was advised he was under arrest and told to 
place his hands behind his back.  This  was shown to be  untrue when viewing the video 
surveillance and listening to the captured audio, yet remained uncorrected by the 
Prosecut or and the pro secutor even referenced both deputies p erjurious statements when 
making fin al arguments.  The audio recording proves that Dixon never audibly said that 
defendant was under arrest, nor did he ever ask Reardon to place his hands behind his back 
when initiating the aggressive takedown and subsequent arrest to follow . Dixon tol d 
Defendan t to move an d Defendant moved.   
 
3. Sheriff Joey East testified to Defendant�s arrival to the Sheriffs Department that da y, 
stating that he pulled in and went to the back of his truck  for a short time  to see if 
Defendant would approach him. However , when the video evidence was slowed down and 
played, the evidence shows  he never went to the back of his vehicle as stated and instead 
rushed inside the sheriffs department clearly intent on avoiding conversation about 
Defendant and his grievance which fell i n the Sheriff�s Jurisdiction.  
 
 
4. A criminal conviction procured by the state prosecuting authorities solely by the use of 
perjured testimony known by them to be perjured and knowingly used by them in order 
to procure the conviction is without due process of law, and in violation of the 
Fourteenth Amendment. Mooney Vs Holohan P. 294 U. S. 112 . 
 
5. The due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment governs any action of a State  
through its legislature, its  courts, or its executive officers, including action through its 
prosecuting officers. P.  294 U. S. 112 . 
 
\n--- Page 3 ---
 
6. When a Conviction is obtained by the presentation of testimony known to the 
prosecuting authorities to have been perjured, Due Process has been violated. This clause 
cannot be deemed to be satisfied by mere notice, and hearing. 
 
7. If the prosecutor knew or should have known that testimony  given to the trial was perjured, 
the conviction must be set aside  if there  is any reasonable likelihood that the false  testimony 
could have affected the judgment of the jury (427 U.S. v Agurs 103, 104) 
 
8. If a state has contrived a conviction, through the pretense of a trial, which in trut h is but 
used as a means of depriving a defendant of liberty through a deliberate, decepti on of the 
court and jury through the presentation  of testimony  known to be perjured,  it is without due 
process of law and in violation of the Four teenth  Amendment.  
 
II. Suppression of Evidence / Failure to Preserve Evidence Crucial to  Trial  
 
 
1. In Brady v. Maryland , the Court held that the suppression by the prosecution of evidence 
favorable to an accused upon request violates due process where the evidence is material 
either to guilt or to punishment, irrespective of the good faith or bad faith of the 
prosecution 
 
2. The heart  of the holding in Brady is the prosecution�s suppression of evidence,  in the face 
of a defense production request, where the evidence is favorable to the accused and is  
material either to guilt or to punishment. Important, then,  are: 
a) Suppression by the prosecution after a request by the defense 
 
b) The evidence�s favorable character for the  defense  
 
c) The materiality of the  evidence.  
 
\n--- Page 4 ---
 
3. In United  States v. Agurs , the Court summarized  and expanded the prosecutor�s obligation 
to disclose to the defense exculpatory evidence in his possession, even in the absence of a 
request, or upon a general request, by defendant 
4. If the defense specifically requested certain evidence and the prosecutor withheld it,  
the conviction must be set asid e if the suppressed evidence might have affected the 
outcome of the trial. (427 Brady v. Maryland 104, 106) 
 
5. If the defense did not make a request at all, or simply asked for all Brady material or for 
anything exculpatory, a duty resides in the prosecution  to reveal to the defense obviously 
exculpatory evidence. (427 US v. Agurs 106, 114) 
 
6. If the prosecutor did not reveal the relevant information, reversal of a conviction may be 
 
required  if the undisclosed evidence creates a reasonable doubt as to the defendant�s guilt  
 
(427 US v Agurs 106, 114) 
 
7. Evidence is considered to be material if there is a reasonable probability that, had the  
evidence been disclosed to the defense, the outcome of the proceeding would have been 
different (US v  Bagley) 
8. The standar d applied in US Vs Bagley  not only applies to exculpatory material,  but also to 
material that would be relevant to the impeachment of  witnesses  
 
9. The Court has held that Brady suppression occurs when the government fails to turn over 
even evidence that is 'known only to police investigators and not to the prosecutor 
 
10. The prosecutor has a duty to learn  of any favorable  evidence  known to others acting  on the 
\n--- Page 5 ---
government's behalf in the case, including police. Youngblood v. West Virginia, 547 U.S. 867 
 
 
III. Sufficiency of Witness Testimony and  Evidence  
 
1. The State has the necessary burden of proving beyond a reasonable doubt every fact 
necessary to constitute the crime  charged. 
 
2. The Defense unwaveringly feels that the  State  of Mississippi  has failed  in proving 
every  fact necessary  to constitute the crime  of DUI charged through the combined 
testimony of Deputy Beavers and Deputy Williford.  Not only do statements made by 
Beavers and Williford contradict  themselves , but the prosecutor in this matter 
seeming ly has condoned the actions of the Lafayette County  Sheriff�s Department  in 
their failure to preserve and maintain key evidence that was requested by Defense for 
the purposes of trial.  Defendant further states the following key facts:  
 
a) Neither  Deputy Beavers  nor Deputy Williford  had a dash camera  in their patrol  vehicle 
b) Neither Deputy wore a body camera at that  time 
 
c) Deputy Williford  testified  to a headlight being out as the cause  of the traffic  stop, not 
swerving or any other sign of an intoxicated driver would generally  show. 
 
d) Defendant verbally expressed his right and desire to speak with an attorney when  
Deputy Beavers insisted on transporting Defendant away from the site of the tra ffic 
stop to the Lafayette County Detention Center for  the purpose of conducting a field 
sobriety  test. 
 
e) Lafayette County Sheriff�s Department retained zero video evidence of the standar d 
field sobriety test being administered in the Sallyport of Lafayette County Sheriffs 
\n--- Page 6 ---
Department

preserved despite timely requests by the  Defendant.

traffic stop to the Lafayette County Detention Center for the purposes of a field 
sobriety test being administered.  Defendant asked to speak to an attorney as he 
thought this was an extremely odd request and Defendant wanted to perform the test 
out in the open in the presence of his spouse in order to have video evidence and an 
eye witness to the test being administered.

test (SFST) in view of a camera/video, Deputy Beavers insisted upon the Sallyport of

i) Defendant�s vehicle was never truly searched by Deputy Williford which Defendant� s 
spouse was an eye witness to.  Even more bizarre was that the Defendant was nev er 
searched until being booked in for the alleged charge of DUI at the Lafayette County 
Detention Center.  Both deputies alleged that the source of odor of burnt marijuana 
came from the vehicle and not the body of Defendant. However, both deputies 
\n--- Page 7 ---
allowed Defendant�s wife to drive his vehicle away after Defendant was placed in the 
back of Beaver�s patrol car for transport to LCDC for a SFST to be administered.  In

that know  illegal substance was present, or simply didn�t care and solely cared about

necessary  to constitute  the crime of Disorderly Conduct and Resisting Arrest, and that the

Sheriffs Dept�.  It didn�t state reserved for Sheriffs Deputies Only, It didn�t state

IV. Defendant was DENIED the right to a Trial by Jury of his peers  
 
1) Defendant hereby raises the constitutionality of such ruling and seeks review of suc h by 
Federal Question to be filed in the US District Court for the Northern District o f 
Mississipp i 
 
2) Miss Code Ann 99 -33-9 specifies the state�s right to a Jury Trial.  It specifies that if potential 
incarceration is more than 6 months the Defendant is entitled that right.  On the contrary,  if 
the potential incarceration is less than 6 months the defendant is not entitled to a Jury  Trial.  
There are two matters in particular which are not addressed and those  issues  include: 
 
a) An alleged crime which sees potential incarceration of 6 months even.  
� The state statute specifically defines more than 6 months as a guaranteed 
right to a trial by jury and less than 6 months potential incarceration as 
there being no right to trial by jury� What about 6 months even? 
 
b) Multiple alleged crimes/charges in which the potential concurrent stacking of 
potenti al incarceration far exceeds that of a total of 6 -month�s time .  
� If potential sentence can be consecutively executed, would this not 
amount to egregious violation of the sixth amendment of the Federal 
Constitution along with Article 3 section 31 of the Stat e of Mississippi 
Constitution detailed out in its Bill of Rights?  
 
3) Defendant stood trial for 9 separate criminal complaints brought by Lafayette County 
Sheriffs Department and Liz Crowder which Defendant has stated since day one wer e 
brought improperly with ill intention, in retaliation on Defendant for his seeking vindica tion 
\n--- Page 11 ---
and justice for a matter in 2017, and in an effort to knowingly interfere with ongoing legal 
matters that presently sit before the MS Supreme Court and Court of Appeals.  
 
V. Defendant alleges that the verdict lacked sufficient evidence and was derived from  knowingly 
perjurious testimony being introduced at trial by the Lafayette County  Prosecutor, and that 
Defendant feels that the verdict reached influenced by mob -domination by the Lafayette 
County Sheriffs Department whom filled one side of the courtroom.  
 
VI. Deprivation of Due Process Rights and violations of 14th Amendment (Equal Protections 
under the laws) without Corrective Process  afforded;  
 
1. That requirement in safeguardi ng the liberty of the citizen against deprivation through the  
action  of the state embodies the fundamental conceptions of justice,  which  lies at the base  of 
our civil and political  institutions (Mooney v. Holohan, 294 U.S. 103, 112 (1935) 
 
2. In the action  of prosecuting officers  on behalf of the state,  those that set laws and enforce 
laws may constitute state action within the purview of the 14th amendment, that 
amendment governs any action of a state, whether through its legislature, through its 
courts, or through its executive or administrative  officers.  
 
3. The principal in enunciated has required state officials to controvert allegation s that 
knowingly false testimony had been used to convict and has upset convictions found to 
have been so procured, extending the  principle. 
(Carter vs Texas, Rogers vs Alabama, Chicago, Burlington, & Quincy vs Chicago)  
 
 
 
4. In each instance, time after time over the past 30 years, the Supreme Court has he ld that the  
14th Amendment cannot tolerate  a state crimin al conviction obtained by the knowing use  of 
\n--- Page 12 ---
false  evidence  (Mooney vs Holohan) It has further elaborated  in subsequent rulings that there  
has been  no deviation from  that established  principle  and there  can be no retreat from that 
principle here. (Navoo vs Illinois, Ohio vs Kansas, Alcorda vs Texas)  
 
5. If the defense  specifically  requested  certain  evidence,  and the prosecutor withheld  it, the 
conviction must be set aside  if the suppressed  evidence  might  have affected  the outcome of 
the trial. (Brady v Maryland) 
 
6. If the defense  did not make a request  at all, or simply  asked  for all Brady  material,  or for 
anything exculpatory, a duty resides in the prosecution to reveal to the defense obvious 
exculpatory evidence. Furthermore, If the prosecutor did not reveal the relevant information,  
reversal  of a conviction may be required  if the undisclosed evidence creates a reasonable 
doubt as to the defendant�s Guilt. (US vs  Augers)  
 
7. A state is not free, to have no corrective  process in which defendants may pursue remedies 
for federal constitutional violations. In Frank v. Mangum , the Court asserted that a 
conviction obtained in a mob -dominated trial was contrary to due process: if the State, 
supplying no corrective process, carries into execution a judgment of death or imprisonme nt 
based  upon a verdict thus produced by mob domination, the State  deprives the accused of his 
life or liberty without due process of law. 
 
 
8. The Supreme Court has stated numerous times that the absence of some form of corrective 
process when the convicted defendant alleges a federal constitutional violation  contravenes the 
Fourteenth Amendment,  and the Court has held that to burden this process, such as by limiting 
the right to petition for habeas corpus, is to deny the convicted defendant his constitutional rights. 
 
\n--- Page 13 ---
VII. Jurisdictional Challenge  
1. In light of the alleged deficiencies and errors which Defendant feels are manife st in nature 
and thus require the resulting conviction to be set aside pursuant to the many w ell 
established cases cited, Defendant would raise a jurisdictional challenge due t o the stated 
federal constitutional rights violations and it would be Defendant�s position and int ention 
to move for removal of these matters to the proper Federal Forum (US District Court) 
should Defendant�s motion go unanswered or denied  
 
WHEREFORE ALL PREMISES CONSIDERED  
Defendant would respectfully request that all findings of guilt be set aside and render ed void due to the 
aforementioned significant issues and grievances primarily regarding insuffici ent evidence/lack of evidence, 
violations of Defenda nt�s Due Process Rights , and the introduction of knowingly perjured testimony without any 
corrective process by the Prosecutor. Defendant prays that th e matt ers withi n be dismissed with prejudice due to 
fraud committed on the court .  In th e alternative, Defendant would seek a new trial by jury of his peers in order to 
assess all evidence and determine guilt. If denied of the relief sought,  Defendant asserts his intent to seek remedy  
through  removal to the US District Court of Northern Mississippi due to Federal Question s arising, for the 
purposes of properly litigating the  Constitutional  issues at hand,  which raise s a proper jurisdictional chal lenge , In 
order  for these matters to tried before a jury panel.  In the interest of Judicial Economy  and apparent confusion 
with bot h Justice  and Circuit court on where Defendant need to file this motion , please  take Judicial Notice of this 
Motion to serve as Defendant�s Notice of Intent to Appeal the Judgement/Order  in order to preserve Defendant �s 
absolute ri ght to appeal.

MOTION FOR DISMISSAL OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE TO TRANSFER 
Comes now. Matthew Reardon, your Defendant, and brings his motion for Dismissal or in the alternative 
to transfer venue to an alternative jurisdiction outside of Lafayette County, Mississippi else Defendant 
hereby gives notice of his intention to immediately petition the US District Court for the Northern 
District of Mississippi for emergency injunctive reliefand the determination of jurisdiction in this cause 
due to all reasons of prejudice and biasness which the Plaintiff in this matter is well aware of and 
shouldn't need to be reminded. Without waiving any of his rights, Defendant/Movant hereby enters 
his plea of NOT GUILTY and hereby gives his Answer. 
1) Movant asserts that this complaint stemmed from what easily appears to show that defendant was 
retaliated upon by Deputy Courtney Dixon whom knew and had issues with the Defendant prior due 
[\0 his filming of a Documentary called "RIDING WITH THE OUTLAW" in an effort to seek 
vindication and justice for the criminal acts aggrieved upon him. Deputy Dixon is seen during one 
of Defendant's more recent video releases approximately two months prior threatening Defendant 
with arrest and further "FISHING" for irrevelant information in order to try to arrest Defendant for 
somereason or another. This all ends with Dixon asking another Deputy if he would like to "WRITE 
UP A REPORT" on the Defendant simply for Defendant being at Sheriff's Department conducting 
an independant investigation and trying to ascertain answers to a particular grievance as to why 
MAJOR ALLEN WILBURN would not produce evidence to an Untrue, Libelous. and Defaming
\n--- Page 12 ---
It was the following day. 12/28/2020 that Defendant drove to Lafayette County Sheriff's Office to 
await the Sheriff in order to confront him in person so as to attempt to compel the assistance of the 
Sheriff, as it is Lafayette County Jurisdiction in which the major issues stem. Defendant parked next 
to Sheriff Joey East's vehicle and awaited the Sheriff outside so he could obtain some type of answer 
from the Sheriff, a Publicly Elected position in which providing such answer should be an obligation 
that most certainly is backed by the protections of Defendant's First Amendment Right to Petition 
the Government for a redress of Grievances. Soon after Deputy Dixon came out with Deputy Omar 
Ahmed. Dixon told Defendant to move his car to which Defendant replied he would happily do so 
once he spoke with the Sheriff, as at this point it was clear the Sheriff was intentionally avoiding 
him. The Deputy then said Defendant had to "Move it now or be arrested for Disorderly Conduet". 
Defendant simply tried to ask how awaiting the Sheriff could be considered Disorderly Conduct 
while in the process of moving his car to another spot when this off-tilt, completely perturbed 
Sheriff�s Deputy told Defendant he was under arrest and proceeded to grabbing Defendant and 
throwing him on the ground using extremely excessive force. Clearly Demonstrating his dislike of 
Defendant in the way he used extremely excessive force in the way he made the arrest. In the 
process, Defendant's head was struck against the ground along with his cell phone being thrown. It 
is the impression of Defendant that this was done in an effort to damage or destroy evidence although 
Defendant can't make such claim with 100% certainty without seeing the video recordings from the 
outdoor cameras. By this time the Sheriff was outside just watching like a spectator would watch a 
sports game. Defendant never resisted arrest even though Mississippi is one of the few states that 
actually allows the resisting of an unlawful, undeserving, and unconstitutional arrest which 
Defendant states in assurance is exactly what this was. Defendant was then restrained for upwards 
ofat least an hour in a restraint chair with his hands tied uncomfortably behind the chair while jailers 
and inmates watched. The Sheriff still refused to talk to Defendant about his grievances.
\n--- Page 13 ---
4) Defendant received a bond amount of $500 the next day. Defendant and his spouse arranged for bond to 
be paid. and upon the bonding agent and Defendant�s Spouse arriving between the hours of Spm-6pm. 
they were turned away after being told Lafayette County Sheriff was invoking some �special authority� 
to personally sign off on Defendants bond and would do so the next day after he personally spoke with 
Defendant. The Sheriff waited until the afternoon on the 30" of December before pulling Defendant into 
aroom and discussing with him his tactics and assertiveness in a court room and in the way he has sought 
justice and vindication, classifying it as wrong when it was this very department along with the malice 
acts of the District Attorney�s Office that outrageously cestroyed the rights and life of defendant just 3 
years prior and once again used fear and arrest as a tactic in their arsenal 1o try to sway a favorable 
outcome. Defendant asserts this amounts to False Arrest and Unlawful Detention among others 
WHEREFORE ALL PREMISES CONSIDERED 
Defendant requests that this cause be immediately dismissed or in the alternative 1o be transferred due 
to the grievances stated within otherwise Defendant counter-claims in the amount of $5.000,000 (Five

JUSTICE COURT JUDGE MICKEY AVANT 
DEFENDANT 
Petition for Writ of Mandamus. 
The plaintiff Matthew Reardon hereby petitions for a writ of mandamus and seeks relief from the 
trial court, trial judge, and ruling made on March 1. 2021 and states that the Defendant Judge 
Mickey Avant acted outside of the Jurisdiction of his office and even authority in this particular 
situation in ordering that the plaintiff cease recording of proceedings brought forth in this court: 
the trial judge did so citing Rule 1.03. The trial judge referenced the UNIFORM RULES OF PROCEDURE 
FOR JUSTICE COURT. Plaintiff stated on numerous occasions that he felt as ifhe couldn�t get a fair 
hearing in the Lafayette County Justice Court due to events that have taken place over the past 3.5 
years all stemming from the crippling decisions made in this very court when Judge Carolyn Bell 
took it on herself to set plaintiff�s bond at $1 50,000 on a felony charge levied against plaintiff in 
May of 2017 which not only lacked probable cause but deprived the plaintiff of his due process 
rights along with his civil liberties by setting bond in a punitive way even when plaintiff asserted 
he had solid proof that he couldn�t have possibly committed such crime. It would have been an 
impossibility. Nonetheless plaintiff was never afforded a preliminary hearing which is a core due 
process right afforded to the accused in which the absence of such within 14 days requires that the
\n--- Page 15 ---
accused be released on his own recognizance. All of this following the plaintiff being held for 
nearly 96 hours prior to receiving a bond hearing which was almost twice the allowed amount of 
time prior to receiving a bond set three times higher than the established maximum, these 
guidelines put in place by the supreme court and readopted in the Mississippi Rules of Criminal 
Procedure less than a year prior. Based on established rules Plaintiff should have received the 
lowest amount for the crime accused being he didn�t get a bond hearing before a judge within the 
required maximum timeframe of 48 hours. All of this played into the stacked and coerced highly 
unfortunate outcome. Plaintiff feels forced to try a case in a court which has gone on tilt before in 
its rulings and decisions and although this trial judge wasn�t the judge presiding on the case in 
2017, plaintiff has assertively claimed reservations and worries that he had including recently 
demonstrating how he feels that he and has even demonstrated Lafayette County Officials and 
Agencies denying Plaintiff the equal protections of the law which are granted not only by Article 
3 of the Mississippi Constitution in its Bill of Rights, but the Federal Constitution which plaintiff 
swore a LIFELONG oath to protect and defend 
In November of 2020 plaintiff was involved in a case in Olive Branch where he was charged with 
contempt despite doing exactly what was asked of him by the court. Fortunately the case was Jjust 
dismissed on 3/1/21 due to plaintiff carrying atape recorder which corroborated exactly that which 
plaintiff claimed. The alleged false arrest is currently on file and pending before the US District 
Court of North Mississippi. referenced by case number 3:20-cv-00327-DMB-JM V. 
It is reasons like this that plaintiff feels it is a paramount need and one that each court should 
allow. In fact Plaintiff is of the impression that this is a request that should be looked at as an 
accommodation and one no different than any accommodation afforded to Americans with
\n--- Page 16 ---
disabilities. The trial judge read off the title to 1.03 in UNIFORM RULES OF PROCEDURE 
FOR JUSTICE COURT. However, after the title, nothing made sense to plaintiff and it almost 
felt to plaintiff that this reading was being falsely read and presented to plaintiff. However, Rule 
1.03 states as follows: 
1:03: USE OF CAMERAS, RECORDING AND ROADCASTING EQUIPMENT The use of 
cameras, recording or broadcasting equipment, or such shall be governed by the Code of Judicial 
Conduct. 
Plaintiff would state that not only does 1:03 lack any language that would bar the use of an 
audio recorder ESPECIALLY in lack of a court reporter/transcriber. This in turn poses 
prejudicial and completely one-sided to the plaintiff where everything is left up to hearsay 
in the absence of proof. 
Plaintiff requested to motion the court for him to be allowed to audio record the hearing and was 
immediately denied further being told something to the effect of �The rules are the rules�. 
Plaintiff hereby quotes RULE 2.03 ADDITIONAL RULES out of the UNIFORM RULES OF 
PROCEDURE FOR JUSTICE COURT which states as follows: No rule of procedure, local or 
otherwise, shall be adopted without the approval of the Mississippi Supreme Court. 
Plaintiff would request answer from this trial court and specifically the trial judge as to if he 
and this court had approval of the Mississippi Supreme Court to establish such a rule that 
adversely can affect the outcome of a legal proceeding, and one which is clearly not spelled
\n--- Page 17 ---
out in the referenced rules of the UNIFORM RULES OF PROCEDURE FOR JUSTICE 
COURT 
Plaintiff would then jump down to RULE 3.01 APPLICABILITY OF RULES in the SAME 
referenced outline: 
Rule 3.01: Rules of criminal procedure in justice court are governed by the Mississippi Rules 
of Criminal Procedure (MRCrP). All rules designated as criminal rules are applicable only 
in criminal cases. General rules are also applicable in criminal cases 
When taking a look at the Mississippi Rules of Criminal Procedure (MRCrP) which just so happens 
to be the true Governing doctrine, and examining the facts of this case, it would appear that the 
rights of the plaintiff on this motion have been effected in a way that could create a material breach 
of even more entitled rights of the plaintiff. 
Rule 1.10 of the Mississippi Rules of Criminal Procedure details Recordation of Proceedings 
where Official Court Reporter not Provided. Any attorney of record or pro se litigant in a court 
which does not provide an official court reporter may record or have recorded any court proceeding 
by audio-recording device or stenographically. Any expenses incident thereto shall be borne by 
the party or parties. This being compounded by plaintiff pointing out flaws and inconsistencies, 
even what plaintiff is under the impression of being an unlawful arrest. Plaintiff has been denied 
access to the closest camera angle of arrest which p]aimiffwholeheanedly believes is an attempt 
to hide or cover up wrongdoings and possibly even criminal wrongdoings, in turn not operating as 
a fair and just system in following plaintiff�s assertions of not being afforded equal protections of 
the laws.
\n--- Page 18 ---
As such, it is for these reasons listed, that plaintiff believes the idea of a fair and impartial trial is 
at an improbability and nearing an impossibility to the plaintiff, as such duress has taken over the 
plaintiff and plaintiff respectfully requests answer from this trial court and trial judge along with 
review of such from the Supreme Court of Mississippi, as plaintiff feels as though not having a 
court reporter, no jury, and no recording equipment potentially paves the way to a constitutional 
crisis and has a habit of removing all fairness from a proceeding that should be comprised of 
fairness. Plaintiff also requests answer as to why Lafayette County is refusing to produce the 
closest camera angle of the arrest and parked vehicle despite resistance on the matter. Plaintiff 
feels that this information is not privileged and is nothing more than Lafayette County Sheriff's 
Department and Lafayette County officials yet again attempting to cover their tracks. 
Plaintiff hereby requestsithat any and all hearings in/this court involving him be pushed back to 
allow proper response and adherence to requests. Ifthis court, the prosecutor, and the state wishes 
to proceed on with this cause and not dismiss in light of information brought forth, then plaintiff 
hereby invokes his right tois peers as guaranteed to him by the state and federa] 
constitutions

IN
 THE COURT OF APPEALS OF MISSISSIPPI

MAT
THEW REARDON

v. 
STA
TE OF MISSISSIPPI

 Appeal from the Circuit Court  
of Lafayette  County, Mississippi  
APPELLANT'S REBUTTAL TO RESPONSE FROM THE STATE
MATTHEW REARDON
Pro-Se Appellant
117 CR 401
Oxford, MS 38655
Telephone: (662) 205-6711
matt@mattreardon.com
COMES NOW The Appellant in this cause, MATTHEW REARDON, and enters his rebuttal to 
the response from State of Mississippi Attorney General's Office entered in this court on September 
20, 2021. In further support of his motion for Peremptory Reversal and the State's response, the 
Appellant submits the following in rebuttal: 
\n--- Page 2 ---
2 I. 
That  not only was the above stated rule regarding setting the initial hearing egregiously ignored 
by the state of Mississippi, but the entire clause in that the minimum bond for release on the 
accused charge would have been $5000 pursuant to Rule 8.1 with a maximum listed at $50,000 
for a crime with a maximum 10 years incarceration.  Not only did Appellants accused crime see a 
maximum of half of that time, but the Judge set appellant's appearance bond at $150,000.Appellant  raises the notion that while he submitted an appellant brief that required listed 
corrections needed, he did still submit his appellant brief.  The brief submitted did in fact list 
manifest error committed on behalf of the State of Mississippi.  The Appellee is attempting to 
have this appeal dismissed based on small technicality error, however, in their response failed to 
address or give answer to the manifest error stated regarding the deprivation of key due process 
rights of the appellant which have been raised.
II.
The first manifest error took place when Appellant was held nearly twice the allowable 
amount of time allotted for a criminal Defendant to be brought before a judge for 
an initial hearing in order for appearance bond to be set in lieu of an appearance 
bond not being issued at the time the warrant was issued. Established due process 
stated in rule 5.1 of the Mississippi Rules of Criminal Procedure addresses this 
in regards to individuals arrested pursuant to a warrant issued on a 
charging affidavit Under Rule 5.1(c)(3), if a person is taken into custody, the person shall 
be taken without unnecessary delay, and in no event later than forty-eight (48) hours after 
arrest, before a judge who shall proceed with an initial appearance. If the person arrested 
is not taken before a judge within forty-eight (48) hours, the person 
detained shall be released on execution of an appearance bond in the minimum 
amount set pursuant to Rule 8 and directed to appear at a specified time and 
place. Rule 5.1(b)(3) conforms to the United States Supreme Court's holdings 
in Gerstein v. Pugh, 420 U.S. 103, 95 S. Ct. 854, 43 L. Ed. 2d 54 (1975), and 
County of Riverside v. McLaughlin, 500 U.S. 44, 111 S. Ct. 1661, 114 L. Ed. 2d 49 (1991).
III.
\n--- Page 3 ---
3  A staggering  3x higher  than a crime  that would  see twice the amount  of time incarceration. Miss.  R. 
Crim.  P. 5.1(C)(2)(a)  and Miss. R. Crim.  P. 8.2 would  support  Appellant's  position  that bond was indeed 
set in a punitive way, violating appellant's 8th Amendment Rights that ensure bond is not set in a punitive 
way, as stated rules required at this point that appellant be released at the minimum amount, being $5,000
IV.
Appellant affirms that he tried to assert he had exculpable evidence of his  innocence at his initial 
hearing to which the judge advised him of his right to remain silent, however, never advised him of 
his right to a preliminary hearing in which he could properly introduce such evidence.  
Miss. R. Crim. P. 5.2 states in felony cases the judge also must:
(1) inform the defendant of the right to a preliminary hearing and the procedure by which that right 
may be exercised; and
(2) When a defendant is charged with commission of a felony, the judge shall also:if requested, set the 
time for a preliminary hearing in accordance with Rule 6.1.
V. 
Appellant was never advised of his right to a preliminary hearing along with one never being set within 14-
days as required in Felony proceedings. This is not discretionary in that: �If a 
prelimina ry hearing has not been commenced within fourteen (14) days as required by subsection (a), unless  
postponed as provided in subsection (d), the defendant shall be released on recognizance."  Miss. R. Crim. 
P. 6.1 Appellant was to be released on recognizance by the 15th day as subsection (d) did not apply to 
appellant. Unless the State can somehow demonstrate how the accused crime would be non-bailable and 
particularly with the exculpatory evidence of appellant's innocence to the accused crime appellant tried to 
introduce at the initial hearing before Judge Bell, the remaining 4-weeks incarceration amount to false 
imprisonment among a plethora of additional violations aggrieved by the appellant.
VI.
The errors committed by the state which in turn compounded and manifested further requires complete 
peremptory reversal of the original criminal cause (LK17-295) or at the bare minimum reversing the trial 
court's order on L20-316 and ordering that a trial be conducted as soon as possible so as not to aggrieve further 
irreparable injury to Appellant
\n--- Page 4 ---
VII.
That dismissing appellant's appeal would aggrieve irreparable harm upon Appellant given the facts 
and merits surrounding the unique series of events directly tied to this appeal, further excusing the 
state of its own neglect in refusing due process rights to the accused and further ignoring mandated 
rules in the Mississippi Rules of Criminal Procedure.  This in turn would deny Appellant equal 
protections under the laws guaranteed under the State and Federal constitution based in whole 
upon state officials knowingly depriving appellant of key rights to begin with.
VIII
Appellant reaffirms the errors in this cause are some of the most manifest in nature and most 
certainly effected the outcome of this cause in pressuring appellant to plea out under immense stress, 
denying him rights, denying him justice, and compounding damages ever since. As such this matter 
meets all necessary required elements for peremptory reversal. Therefore, Appellant respectfully 
requests that this honorable court deny Appellee's unjustified request for dismissal, and expedite a vote 
on his motion for peremptory reversal at their soonest available opportunity in the interest of justice.
VIIII
A party may move to set aside a void judgment pursuant to Rule 60(b)(4) more than six months after entry of 
the judgment if the delay in moving for relief was reasonable. See Ladner v. Logan, 857 So. 2d 764, 770 (Miss. 
2003). A judgment is void if the trial court lacked jurisdiction over the subject matter or the parties or acted 
in a manner inconsistent with due process of law. See Bryant, Inc. v. Walters, 493 So. 2d 933, 938 (Miss. 
1986).
APPELLANT ATTACHES AS EXHIBIT A, A MOTION HE FILED 12/23/2020 UNDER MRCP RULE 60(b) 
WHICH TO DATE HAS BEEN IGNORED AND NOT RULED UPON BY THE TRIAL JUDGE. APPELLANT IS 
OF THE INTERPRETATION THAT THE JUDGE HAD AN OBLIGATION TO ISSUE RULING BASED UPON 
SARTAIN V WHITE 588 SO 2d 204, 211  WHICH STATES:
"The trial court has discretion to grant or deny a Rule 60(b) motion, unless the judgment is void, in which case the 
court is required to set aside the judgment"

Motion for Relief.from Judgment or Order 
Comes now before the court Matthew Reardon, the petitioner /plaintiff, and respectfully moves this 
court for relief pursuant to Rule 60 of The Mississippi Rules of Civil Procedure. Petitioner 
acknowledges that he has an open appeal to the MS Supreme Court based on a denial by this court 
on petitioner 's Post Conviction Relief Motion that he filed on July 7, 2020 (Petition, Exhibits, and 
Denial Attached as EXHIBIT A). The first denial by this court came on July 30, 2020 to which 
petitioner refiled a timely Motion for Re-Hearing under MRCP Rule 59 Before the Honorable Judge 
Kent Smith due to this cause being assigned to Judge Kent Smith. The trial court summarily denied 
this Motion for Re-Hearing under MRCP Rule 59 which was filed based upon newly discovered 
evidence and a plethora of violations aggrieved by petitioner due to the malice and negligent acts of 
the Defendant (Motion and Denial Attached as EXHIBIT B). Up until August 12, 2020 the assigned 
Judge was newly appointed Circuit Judge Kent Smith, yet for reasons unbeknownst to petitioner , 
Judge John Kelly Luther continued denying each of petitioner's motions and petitions despite having 
upfront knowledge of the fraudulent activity in Lafayette County which implemented many. It was 
AFTER the motion for Recusal was filed on August 12, 2020 that the assigned Judge on this cause 
was FRAUDULENTLY changed to Judge John Kelly Luther. This is far from a Clerical Error due 
to the assertion's petitioner had made and provided evidence to regarding conflict of interest 
\n--- Page 7 ---
warranting recusal due to Judge John Kelly Luther's upfront knowledge of crimes that were 
committed and continued to be committed. The denial of petitioner 's motion for recusal filed 
8/12/2020 along with supporting exhibits are attached as EXHIBIT C. Finally, the denial of 
petitioner 's motion to compel and preserve evidence filed August 26, 2020 along with supporting 
exhibits is attached hereto as EXHIBIT D. Petitioner now comes back before this trial court one last 
time prior to the appellate procedure commences and files this Motion for Relief from Judgement or 
Order citing Rule 60(b) which allows relief to be granted to the petitioner in the occurrence of 
Mistakes; Inadvertence; Newly Discovered Evidence; Fraud, etc. 
JURISDICTION AND BASIS FOR MOTION AND CLAIM

demonstrate that he is not required to take leave from the appellate court. MRCP Rule 60 states the 
following to wit: "Leave to make the motion need not be obtained from the appellate court unless 
the record has been transmitted to the appellate court and the action remains pending therein" Rule 
60 -Relie.ffi�om Judgment or Order, Miss. R. Civ. P. 60. The petitioner in this cause brings this motion 
asserting the following meritorious reasons this trial cowt should grant relief under Rule 60: (1) fraud, 
misrepresentation, or other misconduct of an adverse party, (3) newly discovered evidence 
which by due diligence could not have been discovered in time to move for a new trial under 
Rule 59(b ), and (6) any other reason justifying relief from the judgment. It is the opinion of the 
petitioner that the fatal errors that occurred which the Defendant bears the brunt if not sole 
responsibility for are _incurable and in turn have rendered the Judgement in the original 
criminal cause LKl 7-295 to be void and invalid. where the only relief that could possibly remedy

choose to continue denying petitioner the equal protections under the laws of the State and refusing 
\n--- Page 9 ---
him the right to not only restore his life destroyed by way of collusion and lies, and ensuring the 
truly guilty parties are brought to justice. Petitioner hereby conveys unto this trial court the 
following reasons under Rule 60 movant is entitled to relief: 
1) FRAUD COMMITTED BY LAFAYETTE COUNTY OFFICIALS AND EMPLOYEES BY WAY OF 
INTENTIONALLY DEPRIVING PETITIONER IN THIS CAUSE OF DUE PROCESS RIGHTS 
a) MRCP Rule 5 .1 (b )(3) states if a person is taken into custody, the person shall be taken without unnecessary 
delay, and in no event later than forty-eight (48) hours after arrest, before a judge who shall proceed with 
an initial appearance. 
b) The cited rule goes on to state If the person arrested is not taken before a judge within forty-eight ( 48) 
hours, the person detained shall be released on execution of an appearance bond in the minimum 
amount set pursuant to Rule 8 and directed to appear at a specified time and place. Rule 5. l (b )(3) 
conforms to the United States Supreme Court's holdings in Gerstein v. Pugh, 420 U.S. 103, 95 S. Ct. 854, 43 
L. Ed. 2d 54 (1975), and County <~{ Riverside v. McLaughlin , 500 U.S. 44, 111 S. Ct. 1661, 114 L. Ed. 2d 
49 (1991 ). 
c) Due Process rights entitle the defendant in a cause receive a preliminary hearing to determine if there 
is enough evidence warranting the defendant to stand trial..Petitioner was never advised of his right to 
a preliminary hearing. Due to it taking twice the allowable time to bring petitioner before a judge 
for an initial appearance, Petitioner was entitled to his bail being set at the minimum amount on 
the chart in Rule 8, which is $5,000, NOT $150,000. However, Rule 6.1 states that if a preliminar y 
hearing is not commenced within fourteen (14) days as required by subsection (a), and is not postponed 
as allowed by subsection ( d), the defendant shall be released on recognizance, unless the offense is 
non-bailable or release is prohibited by Article 3, Section 29(2) of the Mississippi Constitution of 
\n--- Page 10 ---
1890.THEREFORE, Due to petitioner never being afforded a preliminary hearing, nor a 
preliminary hearing being set within 14 days, The State had an obligation to Release on 
Recognizance, further directing petitioner to appear at a specified time and place pursuant to 
MRCP Rule 5 .1 (b )(3 ). This is not an opinion and it is not a discretionary rule that Defendant can pick 
and choose when to use, but rather this is a statement in fact and is evident by the wording in Rule 6.1 
"The Defendant SHALL be released on recognizance. 
d) The fact that petitioner was never allowed the opportunity upfront to provide proof of his innocence or obtain 
a lower bond amount to regain his freedom to provide for his family is gravely concerning. This is most certainly 
a mitigating factor which provided an unfair, unethical , and unscrupulous advantage to the State, while proving 
to be highly prejudicial to petitioner and is the sole reason that petitioner pied out under coercion and duress at 
the advisement and misrepresentation of his attorney. 
2) PETITIONER WAS DENIED CERTAIN DUE PROCESS RIGHTS GUARANTEED TO ALL CITIZENS 
UNDER ST ATE AND FEDERAL LAWS 
a) Movant/Petitioner was unlawfully held, restricted , suppressed , and confined in the Lafayette County 
Detention Center from the time of his arrest, May 26, 2017 until July 6, 2017 when he pied out to the accused 
crime under duress at the advisement of his attorney. Petitioner received an initial/bond hearing on May 30, 
2017. Defendant was in violation of MRCP Rule 5 .1 (b )(3) by it not bringing petitioner before the court for 
an initial appearance within 48 hours for an appearance bond to be set and in tum steered its own favorable 
outcome by keeping petitioner's appearance bond set at $150,000 for the entire 6 weeks of Petitioner 's 
confinement conveying a completely helpless and hopeless feeling upon petitioner whom needed to be 
released in order to provide care for his minor child 
\n--- Page 11 ---
b) Petitioner was not allowed to speak to offer concrete evidence of his innocence , and further 
petitioner was DENIED the opportunity to obtain a lower bond amount that fell within the 
established guidelines that the petitioner or his family could afford. Movant's bond was set three 
times higher than maximum guidelines allowed despite the State of Mississippi not producing 
so much as a single piece of evidence suggesting it had a valid claim. Petitioner in this cause 
asserted he had evidence to prove his innocence which should have been heard and examined up 
front and most certainly at a Preliminary Hearing. Unfortunately for the petitioner, he never was 
afforded a Preliminary Hearing whether it be through the sole fault of the State, or a shared fault 
of the petitioner 's appointed legal counsel. 
c) A Habeas corpus petition was filed by the public defender commanding the sheriff to bring 
petitioner before the court to show just cause for the illegal confinement of petitioner, and while 
the petition was approved to proceed in forma pauperis, petitioner was never brought before the 
court and was further illegally confined for an additional 4 weeks.

maximum guidelines established less than a year prior by way of the Mississippi Supreme

On 05-24-2017 I, Investigator Jarrett Bundren, was at the office and took a phone call in reference 
to a stalking/harassment complaint. I followed up on the threats and harassment that was reported

other threats and harassment, Todd filed charges on Matt and Matt was arrested and booked in at

IN THE CIRCUIT COURT OF LAFAYETTE COUNTY, MISSISSIPPI 
MATTHEW REARDON PETITIO NER 
vs. cAusE No. L~o-31 (o 
ST A TE OF MISSISSIPPI RESPONDENT 
ORDER DENYING RELIEF REQUESTED 
This cause is before the Court on Petitioner's Motion for Post-Conviction Relief. The 
Court considered the relief requested in the motion pursuant to the Mississippi Uniform Post�
Conviction Collateral Relief Act, Mississippi Code Annotated Sec. 99-39-1, et seq. 
The Petitioner alleges, inter alia, the Respondent "was in direct violation of the 
Petitioner 's l5t, 81\ and 14th constitutional rights at the time the plea was given." The Petitioner 
also alleges he received inadequat e representation. 
After reviewing the documents filed by the Petitioner, the Court finds it plainly appears 
from the face of the motion and prior proceedings that the Petitioner is not entitled to any relief. 
The Court finds the transcript of the plea and sentencing hearing in Petitioner 's criminal cause 
for aggravated stalking (LKl 7-295) belies Petitioner 's claims for relief. 
Therefore , the Court is of the opinion the requested relief is not well taken and is hereby 
DENIED. 
The Circuit Clerk is directed to provide a copy of this Order to the Petitioner .

SO ORDERED and ADJUDGED, this the '30 day of 'J' v) ~ , 2020.

IN THE CIRCUIT COURT OF LAFAYETTE COUNTY, MISSISSIPPI 
MATTHEW REARDON PETITIONER 
vs. CAUSE NO. L20-316 
STATE OF MISSISSIPPI DEFENDANT 
....... , ., .. 
ORDER 
This matter is before the Court on Petitioner's motion titled "Motion for Rehearing Under 
Mississippi Rule 59 Before the Honorable Judge Kent Smith." The Petitioner requests this Court 
reconsider its Order entered July 30, 2020 in which the Court, under Mississippi Code Annotated 
Sec. 99-39-11(2), summarily denied Petitioner's request for post-conviction collateral relief. 
The Petitioner also alleges the undersigned judge should recuse himself from this post-conviction 
matter. 
Toe Court, being fully advised in the premises and having considered the arguments in 
Petitioner's motion for rehearing, finds that the motion is not well taken and is hereby DENIED. 
. �r The Clerk of the Court is directed to provide a copy of this Order to the Petitioner. 
SO ORDERED AND ADJUDGED, this the M day of~ ', 2020.

IN THE CIRCUIT COURT OF LAFA YETI'E COUNTY, MISSISSIPPI 
MATIHEW REARDON PETITIONER 
vs. CAUSE NO. L20-316 
STA TE OF MISSISSIPPI DEFENDANT 
ORDER 
This matter is before the Court on Petitioner's motions titled "Motion for Rehearing 
Under Mississippi Rule 59 Before the Honorable Judge Kent Smith," "Motion for Recusal," and 
"Motion to Compel Evidence ." 
The Petitioner requests this Court reconsider its Order entered July 30, 2020 in which 
the Court, under Mississippi Code Annotated Sec. 99-39-11(2), summarily denied Petitioner's 
request for post-conviction collateral relief. The Court finds the motion for reconsideration is 
not well taken and is hereby DENIED. Consequently , the Court finds Petitioner 's Motion to 
Compel Evidence to be MOOT. 
Additionally, the Petitioner alleges the undersigned judge should recuse himself from this 
post-conviction matter. The Court finds the Petitioner's request for recusal to be without merit 
and hex:eby DENIED. 
The Clerk of the Court is directed to provide a copy of this Order to the Petitioner. 
SO ORDERED AND ADJUDGED, this !he ~ay of~. , 2020.

Statement of the Issues 
1. Whether The trial court erred in not granting evidentiary hearing based on allegations of fact in post 
conviction relief and subsequent motions

corrupt involvement surrounding the details of this cause 
a) Was intentional and avoidable misconduct by trial judge to blame. 
b) Did altering the names of the assigned judge rise to the level of fraud committed during the 
onset of an active case

before Justice Court Judge Carolyn Bell in order for bond to be set 
4. Whether the trial court erred and in fact committed plain and reversible error when Reardon was 
never presented or afforded a preliminary hearing 
5. Whether the trial court erred in setting Mr Reardon's appearance bond at $150,000 for a crime Mr 
Reardon had claimed he had exculpatory evidence proving the alleged crime as one of an 
impossibility to have been committed

bond at an astronomical, unconstitutional, and in a punitive way based on the fact that Mr. Reardon 
had never before been accused of a felony crime, nor were any injuries were inflicted upon the 
accuser/alleged victim

which protects against excessive bail and cruel and unusual punishment inflicted

Amendment Rights

combined with the undiagnosed mental illness ofBiPolar.

this accusation warranted release on personal recognizance in the absence of such

Statement of the Case 
Matthew Reardon pied out under extreme duress due to the trial court and Lafayette 
County Officials acting in a manner inconsistent with due process of law. Reardon submitted 
a Post Conviction Relief in July of 2020 seeking to toss his plea due to due process rights 
deprivation, fraud committed, malfeasant legal representation, and new exculpatory evidence 
arising. Reardon's motion was denied I as well as subsequent motions further detailing 
everything out. He files this direct appeal challenging the legality of both his plea and the 
sentence based on the grounds set forth herein, and ultimately seeks peremptory reversal due 
to manifest errors which justify such. At the very minimum, Reardon seeks an evidentiary 
hearing to be granted in order to properly present the facts and details of his case. 
Statement of Facts 
Mr. Reardon was charged with Aggravated stalking on May 26, 2017 alleging that on or 
before May 8th, 201 7 he put Todd Lynch in fear of his life with an AR-15. Reardon was held 
on a $150,000 bond set by Justice Court Judge Carolyn Bell and stayed incarcerated for six 
weeks time until he pied out under duress in order to regain his freedom to support his minor 
child. Reardon has a bill of sale proving he didn't purchase said weapon until May 20th, 2017 
in Picayune, MS from an individual named Larry Watkins. Reardon tried to assert he had a bill 
of sale proving his innocence at his initial hearing and was further denied the ability to speak. 
Reardon was never afforded a preliminary hearing nor advised of his right, however Reardon' s 
wish to present evidence by way of a bill of sale should have been received by this justice court 
\n--- Page 7 ---
judge as Reardon's want and need for a preliminary hearing. However no preliminary hearing 
was ever set nor was Reardon's bond lowered from the original amount of $150,000. This is 
three times higher than the stated maximum in the newly revised MS Rules of Criminal 
Procedure. asserting the following meritorious reasons this trial court should grant relief under Rule 
60: (1) fraud, misrepresentation, or other misconduct of an adverse party, (3) newly 
discovered evidence which by due diligence could not have been discovered in time to 
move for a new trial under Rule 59(b ), and ( 6) any other reason justifying relief from the 
judgment. It is the opinion of the Appellant that the fatal errors that occurred which the 
Defendant bears the brunt if not sole responsibility for are incurable and in turn have 
rendered the Judgement in the original criminal cause LK17-295 to be void and invalid. 
where the only relief that could possibly remedy the fraud and misconduct committed would be 
a complete reversal of the plea and judgement or the setting of a hearing on facts and evidence. 
Appellant further affirms that he has continued to be victimized by false accusations and 
frivolous legal proceedings initiated to harass, delay, and ultimately influence other legal 
proceedings including this one and that Appellant has been refused access and visitation to his 
minor child on knowingly false statements made by Phyllis Crowder-Kester and her attorneys. 
Appellant has provided evidence of a plethora of crimes actively being committed which rise to 
criminal conspiracy, RICO, and The Filing False Instruments which shows the intentional 
commission of Fraud. Defendant has refused to provide Appellant the equal protections of the 
laws under the 14th amendment of the United States Constitution while adamantly refusing to 
allow an evidentiary hearing or even examining and ascertaining the jaw dropping facts of fraud 
being committed and refusing to reprimand those who are responsible for everything. Most 
appalling is that the Defendant in this cause has seemingly praised its actors for treasonous 
crimes against humanity which the actions detailed out have led to by Defendant refusing to 
\n--- Page 8 ---
contain and cure its default and defect against not only its own established rules, laws, and state 
constitution but the United States Constitution which Appellant swore a lifelong oath to his 
country to protect and defend. It is for these reasons listed that Appellant conveys in the most 
respectful yet adamant of ways that denying him relief he seeks under the facts and valid 
reasons listed in this motion and encompassing this entire case which the Defendant chose to 
initiate on false pretenses would completely deny him of the equal protections under the laws 
afforded by the 14th Amendment to the United States Constitution. This in turn would aggrieve 
permanent and irreparable harm on the Appellant and his family, demonstrating a complete 
refusal by the Defendant to act ethically and in a morally satisfying way, forever severing all 
public trust and integrity. 
APPELLANT STATES THAT FRAUD COMMITTED BY LAFAYETTE COUNTY OFFICIALS 
AND EMPLOYEES, INTENTIONALLY DEPRIVED HIM OF HIS DUE PROCESS RIGHTS 
MRCP Rule 5.l(b)(3) states if a person is taken into custody, the person shall be taken without 
unnecessary delay, and in no event later than forty-eight (48) hours after arrest, before a judge 
who shall proceed with an initial appearance. 
The cited rule goes on to state If the person arrested is not taken before a judge within forty-eight 
(48) hours, the person detained shall be released on execution of an appearance bond in the 
minimum amount set pursuant to Rule 8 and directed to appear at a specified time and place. 
Rule 5.l(b)(3) conforms to the United States Supreme Court's holdings in Gerstein v. Pugh, 420 U.S. 
103, 95 S. Ct. 854, 43 L. Ed. 2d 54 (1975), and County of Riverside v. McLaughlin , 500 U.S. 44, ill 
S. Ct. 1661, 114 L. Ed. 2d 49 (1991). 
\n--- Page 9 ---
Due Process rights entitle the defendant in a cause receive a preliminary hearing to determine if 
there is enough evidence warranting the defendant to stand trial..Appellant was never advised of 
his right to a preliminary hearing. Due to it taking twice the allowable time to bring 
Appellant before a judge for an initial appearance, Appellant was entitled to his bail being 
set at the minimum amount on the chart in Rule 8, which is $5,000, NOT $150,000. 
However, Rule 6.1 states that if a preliminary hearing is not commenced within fourteen (14) 
days as required by subsection (a), and is not postponed as allowed by subsection (d), the 
defendant shall be released on recognizance, unless the offense is non-bailable or release is 
prohibited by Article 3, Section 29(2) of the Mississippi Constitution of 1890.THEREFORE, 
Due to Appellant never being afforded a preliminary hearing, nor a preliminary hearing 
being set within 14 days, The State had an obligation to Release on Recognizance, further 
directing Appellant to appear at a specified time and place pursuant to MRCP Rule 5.l(b)(3). 
This is not an opinion and it is not a discretionary rule that Defendant can pick and choose when 
to use, but rather this is a statement in fact and is evident by the wording in Rule 6.1 "The 
Defendant SHALL be released on recognizance. 
The fact that Appellant was never allowed the opportunity upfront to provide proof of his innocence or 
obtain a lower bond amount to regain his freedom to provide for his family is gravely concerning. This 
is most certainly a mitigating factor which provided an unfair, unethical, and unscrupulous advantage to 
the State, while proving to be highly prejudicial to Appellant and is the sole reason that Appellant pled 
out under coercion and duress at the advisement and misrepresentation of his attorney. 
APPELLANT WAS DENIED CERTAIN DUE PROCESS RIGHTS GUARANTEED TO ALL 
CITIZENS UNDER STATE AND FEDERAL LAWS

RESPONSE IN OPPOSITION TO  
MOTION FOR PEREMPTORY REVERSAL  
 
       
LYNN FITCH  
      Attorney General of Mississippi  
 
      BARBARA BYRD  
      Special Assistant Attorney General  
      Mississippi Bar No. 104233   
      P.O. Box 220  
      Jackson, Mississippi 39205 -0220

  E-Filed Document                Sep 20 2021 16:52:40                2020-CP-01259-COA                Pages: 5
\n--- Page 2 ---
2 
 The State of Mississippi moves this Court to deny the Appellant�s 
Motion for Peremptory Reversal . In further support of this Motion, the 
State submits the following:  
I. 
The Appellant filed his brief on August 20, 2021, and a brief non -
compliance letter was issued the same day . The letter indicated that the 
Appellant  had failed to  (1) include the trial judge in his certificate of 
service, (2) submit a copy of his brief in disk -form as required by Rule 
28(n) of the Mississippi Rules of Appellate Procedure, and  (3) submit 
record excerpts in compliance with Rule 30.  
II. 
The Appell ant requested additional time to file his corrections and  
was granted seven days , until September 16 , 2021, to file.  
III. 
On September 17, 2021, the Appellant filed a Motion for 
Peremp tory Reversal and alternatively requested an extension of time to 
file his corrections  while simultaneously asking the Court to expedite the 
consideration of his appeal .  
 
\n--- Page 3 ---
3 
 IV. 
 
 This Court should deny the Appellant�s request for peremptory 
reversal. He has cited no authority which supports his request. Further, 
his appeal is not properly before this Court because he has failed to 
comply with the mandates of the Mississippi Rules of Appellate 
Procedure.  If and when Reardon files his corrected  brief, the State should 
have an opportunity to respond. M.R.A.P. 31(b).  
V. 
Because the Appellant has failed to show why he has yet to cure the 
deficiencies of his appeal , this Court should deny his request for 
additional time to do so. If the Court denies  the Appellant�s second 
request for time, it should also dismiss his appeal. See M.R.A.P. 31 (d) and 
M.R.A.P. 2(a)(2).  
VI. 
This Court should deny the Appellant�s request for an expedited 
appeal. Reardon appeals the denial of his motion  for post -conviction 
relief;  thus, this case is civil in nature . Miss. Code Ann. � 99 -39-7. Rule 
23(a) of the Mississippi Rules of Appellate Pro cedure provides that 
�[e]xcept  as may be provided by special order, all civil cases will be 
\n--- Page 4 ---
4 
 submitted in the order in which  they stand on the docket. � The comment 
to Rule 23(a) lists certain categories  of cases that are statutorily entitled 
to preferential  docketing , none of which apply here. Accordingly, this 
Court should deny the Appellant�s request for expedition.

RULE 1.09 NOTIFICATION OF RELEVANT PENDING CASES  
�When counsel in a cause is aware of a case pending in another court of this or any other state or 
federal jurisdiction, which lik ely may affect the subject matter or jurisdiction of the cause pending in 
the Chancery Court, such counsel shall immediately notify  the Chancery Court by written notice 
docketed for such other cause .  
The Plaintiff and  her counsel have  not only failed , but have  contumaciously failed to comply with this 
crucial rule of the UNIFORM CHANCERY COURT RULES , and in the act  of concealing their 
already contuma cious  acts have now  seemingly convinced this court to challenge the jurisdiction of 
nearly every item on trial in  Lafayette County Justice Court two separate times,  thus subjecting 
Defendant in both venues to Double Jeopardy. This Amounts to a  clear violation of Defendants 
constitutional rights , both State and Federal ; and a open refusal of his due process rights by asserting 
his guilt and making him prove his innocence , while by every definition exploiting the Defendant and 
in so doing, exploiting the minor child.  
Voluntary dismissal is allowed under M.R.C.P. 41 at any time prior to service by the adverse party of 
an answer or summary judgment, when a civil case is so dismissed and then refiled immediately 
thereafter with no substantial change in the parties or claims, such practice, as an example, may be taken as a willful violation of this rule. Willful violation of this rule may constitute an offense subject 
to suspension and other discipline under Rule 3.4(c) of the Rules of Professional Conduct. Sanctions authorized by thi s rule are cumulative to discipline under the Rules of Professional Conduct. 
 
\n--- Page 12 ---
 
RULE 2.02 PLEADINGS MUST BE FILED BEFORE PRESENTED  
All pleadings, accounts and other papers in any action shall be filed with the Clerk of the proper 
Court before being presented to the Chancellor. If to do so would inflict undue hardship on the attorney, or in emergency matters, the papers may be presented to the Chancellor and marked filed by him as provided in M.R.C.P. 5(e). Thereafter, the said  papers shall be forthwith transmitted by 
the attorney to the proper Clerk 
IN MULTIPLE INSTANCES BY MOTION BEFORE THIS COURT, MOVANT RAISED 
LARGE GRIEVANCES ABOUT  THE FACT THAT ORDERS INCLUDING AGREED TO 
ORDERS WERE GENERATED, AD JUDGED, AND FILED WITH THE OPPOSING 
COUNSEL SIGNATURE AND THE JUDGES SIGNATURE, YET UNSIGNED BY MOVANT THROUGH NO REFUSAL TO SIGN BY MOVANT.  
 
RULE 2.0 3 NO BLANKS IN PLEADINGS  addresses this error, which to this day has remained 
raised via multiple filed documents and motions with this court, yet remains unaddressed and now 
manifest error  
RULE 2.0 3 of the Uniform Chancery Court Rules States the following:  
�No blanks shall be contained in any pleading.�  
 
RULE 2.06 BLANKS IN PLEADINGS MUST BE FILLED IN  
All blanks contained in any pleading must be properly filled in according to the fact or facts before being filed with the clerk or presented for consideration by the Court or Chancellor. If the pleader 
\n--- Page 13 ---
does not know, and is unable to learn, the necessary fact or facts to enable him to fill in such blanks 
accurately, he must so state in his pleading 
 
 
 
RULE 1.11 MOTIONS FOR RECUSAL OF JUDGES  
Any party may move for the recusal of a judge of the chancery court if it appears that the judge�s 
impartially might be questioned by a reasonable person knowing all the circumstances, or for 
other grounds provided in the Code of Judicial Conduct or otherwise as provided by law. A motion seeking recusal shall be filed with an affidavit of the party or the party�s attorney setting forth the factual basis underlying the asserted grounds for recusal and declari ng that the motion is filed in 
good faith and that the affiant truly believes the facts underlying the grounds stated to be true. 
Such motion shall, in the first instance, be filed with the judge who is the subject of the motion 
within 30 days following no tification to the parties of the name of the judge assigned to the case; 
or, if it is based upon facts which could not reasonably have been known to the filing party within such time, it shall be filed within 30 days after the filing party could reasonably  discover the facts 
underlying the grounds asserted. The subject judge shall consider and rule on the motion within 30 days of the filing of the motion, with hearing if necessary. If a hearing is held, it shall be on the record in open court. The denial of  a motion to recuse is subject to review by the Supreme Court 
on motion of the party filing the motion as provided in M.R.A.P. 48B.

The Judge s hould not accept without question the affiant�s mere conclusion 
that the person whose arrest is sought has committed a crime�

RULE 1.06 OF THE UNIFORM CHANCERY COURT RULES DISCUSSES 
\n--- Page 15 ---
ASSIGNMENT OF CASES  WHERE THERE MAY BE A CONFLICT OR 
CHALLENGE OF JURISDICTION 
(A)  In multi -judge districts and courts, all civil cases shall be assigned immediately on the filing of 
the complaint by such method which shall insure that the assignment shall be random, that no 
discernable pattern of assignment exists, and that no person shall know to whom the case will be assigned until it has been assigned. If an attorney or party shall attempt to manipulate or defeat the purpose of this rule, the case shall be reassigned to the judge who would have otherwise received the assignment. If the judge who would have received the case under an assignment in compliance with this rule cannot be determined, a new assignment in compliance with the rule shall be made, excluding the judge t o whom it was incorrectly assigned. Sanctions, 
including costs and attorney�s fees, may be imposed by that judge on reassignment. Such sanctions may also include suspension from practice in the court imposing them for not more than 30 days and referral to the Bar for further discipline. 
(B)  Decisions regarding this rule shall be subject to review by the Supreme Court under M.R.A.P. 21, and appropriate stays shall be entered by the trial court to allow such review. 
(C) In districts where motion days are set in advance with judges specifically assigned, preliminary procedural matters and those matters enumerated under M.R.C.P. 81(d)(1) and (2) may be submitted to the judge assigned such duties, notwithstanding the fact t hat the case has been 
assigned to another judge. Furthermore, by local rule approved by the Supreme Court, the trial court may make special provisions accommodating local needs of  economy and efficiency which 
might otherwise be at variance with this rule.
