RULE 1.09 NOTIFICATION OF RELEVANT PENDING CASES  
�When counsel in a cause is aware of a case pending in another court of this or any other state or 
federal jurisdiction, which lik ely may affect the subject matter or jurisdiction of the cause pending in 
the Chancery Court, such counsel shall immediately notify  the Chancery Court by written notice 
docketed for such other cause .  
The Plaintiff and  her counsel have  not only failed , but have  contumaciously failed to comply with this 
crucial rule of the UNIFORM CHANCERY COURT RULES , and in the act  of concealing their 
already contuma cious  acts have now  seemingly convinced this court to challenge the jurisdiction of 
nearly every item on trial in  Lafayette County Justice Court two separate times,  thus subjecting 
Defendant in both venues to Double Jeopardy. This Amounts to a  clear violation of Defendants 
constitutional rights , both State and Federal ; and a open refusal of his due process rights by asserting 
his guilt and making him prove his innocence , while by every definition exploiting the Defendant and 
in so doing, exploiting the minor child.  
Voluntary dismissal is allowed under M.R.C.P. 41 at any time prior to service by the adverse party of 
an answer or summary judgment, when a civil case is so dismissed and then refiled immediately 
thereafter with no substantial change in the parties or claims, such practice, as an example, may be taken as a willful violation of this rule. Willful violation of this rule may constitute an offense subject 
to suspension and other discipline under Rule 3.4(c) of the Rules of Professional Conduct. Sanctions authorized by thi s rule are cumulative to discipline under the Rules of Professional Conduct. 
 
\n--- Page 3 ---
 
RULE 2.02 PLEADINGS MUST BE FILED BEFORE PRESENTED  
All pleadings, accounts and other papers in any action shall be filed with the Clerk of the proper 
Court before being presented to the Chancellor. If to do so would inflict undue hardship on the attorney, or in emergency matters, the papers may be presented to the Chancellor and marked filed by him as provided in M.R.C.P. 5(e). Thereafter, the said  papers shall be forthwith transmitted by 
the attorney to the proper Clerk 
IN MULTIPLE INSTANCES BY MOTION BEFORE THIS COURT, MOVANT RAISED 
LARGE GRIEVANCES ABOUT  THE FACT THAT ORDERS INCLUDING AGREED TO 
ORDERS WERE GENERATED, AD JUDGED, AND FILED WITH THE OPPOSING 
COUNSEL SIGNATURE AND THE JUDGES SIGNATURE, YET UNSIGNED BY MOVANT THROUGH NO REFUSAL TO SIGN BY MOVANT.  
 
RULE 2.0 3 NO BLANKS IN PLEADINGS  addresses this error, which to this day has remained 
raised via multiple filed documents and motions with this court, yet remains unaddressed and now 
manifest error  
RULE 2.0 3 of the Uniform Chancery Court Rules States the following:  
�No blanks shall be contained in any pleading.�  
 
RULE 2.06 BLANKS IN PLEADINGS MUST BE FILLED IN  
All blanks contained in any pleading must be properly filled in according to the fact or facts before being filed with the clerk or presented for consideration by the Court or Chancellor. If the pleader 
\n--- Page 4 ---
does not know, and is unable to learn, the necessary fact or facts to enable him to fill in such blanks 
accurately, he must so state in his pleading 
 
 
 
RULE 1.11 MOTIONS FOR RECUSAL OF JUDGES  
Any party may move for the recusal of a judge of the chancery court if it appears that the judge�s 
impartially might be questioned by a reasonable person knowing all the circumstances, or for 
other grounds provided in the Code of Judicial Conduct or otherwise as provided by law. A motion seeking recusal shall be filed with an affidavit of the party or the party�s attorney setting forth the factual basis underlying the asserted grounds for recusal and declari ng that the motion is filed in 
good faith and that the affiant truly believes the facts underlying the grounds stated to be true. 
Such motion shall, in the first instance, be filed with the judge who is the subject of the motion 
within 30 days following no tification to the parties of the name of the judge assigned to the case; 
or, if it is based upon facts which could not reasonably have been known to the filing party within such time, it shall be filed within 30 days after the filing party could reasonably  discover the facts 
underlying the grounds asserted. The subject judge shall consider and rule on the motion within 30 days of the filing of the motion, with hearing if necessary. If a hearing is held, it shall be on the 
record in open court. The denial of  a motion to recuse is subject to review by the Supreme Court 
on motion of the party filing the motion as provided in M.R.A.P. 48B.

RULE 1.06 OF THE UNIFORM CHANCERY COURT RULES DISCUSSES 
\n--- Page 6 ---
ASSIGNMENT OF CASES  WHERE THERE MAY BE A CONFLICT OR 
CHALLENGE OF JURISDICTION 
(A)  In multi -judge districts and courts, all civil cases shall be assigned immediately on the filing of 
the complaint by such method which shall insure that the assignment shall be random, that no 
discernable pattern of assignment exists, and that no person shall know to whom the case will be assigned until it has been assigned. If an attorney or party shall attempt to manipulate or defeat the purpose of this rule, the case shall be reassigned to the judge who would have 
otherwise received the assignment. If the judge who would have received the case under an 
assignment in compliance with this rule cannot be determined, a new assignment in compliance 
with the rule shall be made, excluding the judge t o whom it was incorrectly assigned. Sanctions, 
including costs and attorney�s fees, may be imposed by that judge on reassignment. Such sanctions may also include suspension from practice in the court imposing them for not more than 30 days and referral to the Bar for further discipline. 
(B)  Decisions regarding this rule shall be subject to review by the Supreme Court under M.R.A.P. 21, and appropriate stays shall be entered by the trial court to allow such review. 
(C) In districts where motion days are set in advance with judges specifically assigned, preliminary procedural matters and those matters enumerated under M.R.C.P. 81(d)(1) and (2) may be submitted to the judge assigned such duties, notwithstanding the fact t hat the case has been 
assigned to another judge. Furthermore, by local rule approved by the Supreme Court, the trial court may make special provisions accommodating local needs of  economy and efficiency which 
might otherwise be at variance with this rule.

IN THE CIRCUIT COURT OF LAFAYETTE COUNT\\ MISSISSIPPI 
MATTHEW REARDON � PETITIONER 
vs. cAusE No. L~o ... 31 (o 
STATE OF MISSISSIPPI RESPONDENT 
ORDER DENYING RELIEF REQUESTED 
This cause is before the Court on Petitioner's Motion for Post-Conviction Relief. The 
Court c.onsidered the relief requested in the motion pursuant to the Mississippi Uniform Post�
Conviction Collateral Relief Act, Mississippi Code Annotated Sec. 99-39-1. et seq. 
The Petitioner alleges, inter alia, the Respondent "was in direct violation of the 
Petitioner's 1s1, 8th, and 14th constitutional rights at the time the plea was given." The Petitioner 
also alleges he received inadequate representation. 
After reviewing the documents filed by the Petitioner, the Court finds it plainly appears 
from the face of the motion and prior proceedings that the Petitioner is not entitled to.any relief. 
The Court finds the transcript of the plea and sentencing hearing in Petitioner's criminal cause 
for aggravated stali.::ing (LKI 7~295) belies Petitioner's claims for relief. 
Therefore, the Court is of the opinion the req�ested relief is not well taken and is hereby 
DENIED. 
The Circuit Clerk is directed to provide a copy :of this Order to the Petitioner.

ORDER 
Th.is matter is before the Court on Petitioner's motion titled '~Motion for Rehearing Under 
Mjssissippi Rule 59 Before the Honorable Judge Kent Smith." The Petitioner requests this Court 
reconsider its Order entered July 30, 2020 in which the Court, under Mississippi Code Annotated 
Seo. 99-,39-11(2), summarily denied Petitioner's request for post-conviction collateral relief. 
The Petitiq11er also alleges the undersigned judge should recuse himself from this. post-conviction 
matter. 
The Court, being fully advised in the premises and having COJlSidered the arguments in 
Petitioner's motion for rehearing, finds that the motion is not well taken and is hereby DENIED. 
. � '{. The Clerk of the Court is directed to provide a copy of this Order to the Petitioner. 
SO ORDERED AND ADJUD<JED, this the /,;2 day oJ~ ', 20:ZO �

followed as outlined in the Mississippi Code for post-conviction relief. If you wish to have the

conviction Relief Act.

      IN THE THIRD CIRCUIT  COURT FOR LAFAYETTE COUNTY MISSISSIPPI  
 
STATE OF MISSISSIPPI  
Vs       Cause No. LK17 -295 
   MATT REARDON   
___________________________________________________________________________
Motion for Relief from Judgment or Order  
_________________________________________________________________________________  
 
Comes now before the court Matthew Reardon, the Movant, and now respectfully moves this court 
under this cause number grant Movant�s Motion for Relief from judgement or order pursuant to Rule 60 
(b) (4) of The Mississippi Rules of Civil Procedure.  Movant acknowledges that he has an open appeal in 
the MS Court of Appeals which addresses the Post -Conviction Relief filed in this court on July 7, 2020.  
However, the original criminal cause was not properly addressed in matters Movant now feels were the best 
of ways. Pursuant to the Mississippi Rules of Civil Procedure, the true governing doctrine on Movant�s 
Post Conviction Relief, the Judgement on which he filed his post -conviction relief upon should have been 
rendered null, void, and invalid, which in turn ha s burdened the Movant on perfecting his appeal of the 
denied post -conviction relief. Movant has long alleged this complaint was FRAUDULENT, without 
justification, merit, and evidence since day one and Movant properly raises the constitutionality of 
Movant�s plea in a situation where Movant was denied basic due process rights/constitutional rights over 
the entire 6 -weeks leading up to his plea entered in criminal cause LK17 -295.  Movant is now asking for 
this court to promptly do so and  declare it so based on the cited, well settled law brought forth in this 
motion on principal, else Movant hereby invokes his rights under the Williams -Jennings Reservation and 
places the State of Mississippi and all liable parties of the violations of not only the State of Mi ssissippi�s 
own statutes, rules and laws, but significant violations of Federal Law through a multitude of Federal 
Constitutional Civil Rights violations as well, which Movant will detail out in this motion.  Movant states 
his motion will show that the Sta te of Mississippi has had an obligation and has known of its obligation to 
ensure the accused are afforded Due Process in order to safeguard the constitutional rights of it�s citizens 
\n--- Page 2 ---
and to preserve equality and integrity.  The State of Mississippi is awa re of the consequences derived from 
depriving a citizen of his/her due process rights, including the requirement in and that in the event it is made 
aware of constitutional/due process right violations that it is mandated, and further has an absolute 
obligation to provide corrective process pursuant to not only its own stated guidelines, rules, and laws but 
well-established Federal Case Law as well.  
MOVANT HEREBY MAKES HIS ARGUMENT AND OFFERS IN SUPPORT OF HIS MOTION:  
 
I. A party may move to set aside a void judgment pursuant to MRCP Rule 60(b)(4) more than six 
months after the entry of a judgment if the delay in moving for relief was reasonable.  
See Ladner v. Logan, 857 So. 2d 764, 770 (Miss. 2003)  
 
II. A judgment is void if the trial court lacked jurisdiction o ver the subject matter or the parties or acted 
in a manner inconsistent with due process of law.  See Bryant, Inc. v. Walters, 493 So. 2d 933, 938 
(Miss. 1986).  
 
III. The trial court has discretion to grant or deny a Rule 60(b) motion, unless the judgment is void, in 
which case the court is  required to set aside the judgment. See Sartain v. White, 588 So. 2d 204, 211 
(Miss. 1991).  The key word in this being the court is REQUIRED TO SET ASIDE A VOID 
JUDGEMENT.    
 
IV. On Friday May 26, 2017 approximate ly between the hours of 3pm -4pm Reardon was taken into 
custody in Oxford, MS City Square by Lafayette County Sheriffs Department and charged with 
Aggravated Stalking. A warrant had allegedly been issued to make the arrest, although Reardon was 
never show a  warrant at the time of arrest nor was he ever informed of his charge until he was booked 
at the Lafayette County.  The Criminal information in this matter states the appellant: Did feloniously 
commit the crime of stalking as defined in section 97 -3-107 (1) and did so while displaying a deadly 
weapon, to wit: an AR15 rifle with, the intent to place Todd and or Ashley Lynch, in reasonable fear 
\n--- Page 3 ---
of great bodily injury, a violation of section 97 -3-107(2) of the Mississippi Code of 1972, as amended, 
WHICH OFFENS E IS PUNISHABLE BY NOT MORE THAN 5 YEARS IMPRISONMENT 
AND A $3,000 FINE OR BOTH.  
 
V. Appellant did not receive an Initial Hearing in which Appearance Bond was Issued until 
Tuesday May 30th, 2017 between the estimated hours of 9am -10am. At this point, Reardon 
was escorted across from the backside of the Lafayette County Detention Center to the 
Lafayette County Justice Court for an Initial Hearing before Judge Carolyn Pettis -Bell. From 
the very beginning, this amounted to False Imprisonment and over -detention in  that pursuant 
to Mississippi Law and Federal Law Governing the Due Process Rights of The Accused, 
Lafayette County and The State of Mississippi had A MAXIMUM OF 48 -HOURS TIME to 
set an Initial Hearing and bring the accused before a Judge in order for Bond  to be set. This 
is no new law or requirement, and it was an obligation that Lafayette County Sheriff's 
Department had known upfront of its duties to comply with.  
 
VI. Miss Rules of Criminal Procedure Rule 5.1(b)(3) details out that if a person is taken into c ustody, the 
person shall be taken without unnecessary delay,  AND IN NO EVENT  later than forty -eight (48) 
hours after arrest, before a judge who shall proceed with an initial appearance. If the person arrested 
is not taken before a judge within forty -eight (48) hours, THE PERSON DETAINED SHALL BE 
RELEASED ON EXECUTION OF AN APPEARANCE BOND IN THE MINIMUM AMOUNT 
SET PURSUANT TO RULE 8 AND DIRECTED TO APPEAR AT A SPECIFIED TIME AND 
PLACE. Rule 5.1(b)(3) conforms to the United States Supreme Court�s holdings in  Gerstein v. Pugh, 
420 U.S. 103, 95 S. Ct. 854, 43 L. Ed. 2d 54 (1975), and County of Riverside v. McLaughlin, 500 
U.S. 44, 111 S. Ct. 1661, 114 L. Ed. 2d 49 (1991).  
 
 
\n--- Page 4 ---
VII. According to the range chart on Miss Rules of Criminal Procedure Rule 8.2, which was 
updated earlier that same year (2017) , a bond for an alleged crime that could receive UP 
TO 10-YEARS INCARCERATION should be set in the range of $5,000 - $50,000. Appellant 
reinforces that he was not charged with any capital crime or offense which is a non -bailable 
offense and that in fact the crime he was accused of receives a maximum of 5 -YEARS 
INCARCERATION.  As Such, THE MINIMUM BOND AMOUNT PURSUANT TO RULE 8 
THAT THE MOVANT �SHALL� HAVE BEEN RELEASED ON WOULD HAVE BEEN $5,000!

constitutes a DIRECT violation of Appellant's 8th Amendment  Federal Constitutional Rights along 
with Art. 3, � 29  of the Mississippi Constitution which explicitly prohibits the setting of Excessive 
bail and/or setting bail/bond in a punitive way

ALONG WITH ONE NEVER BEING SET WITHIN 14 DAYS AS REQUIRED IN FELONY 
PROCEEDINGS . This is not a discretionary rule as Miss. R. Crim. P. 6.1 states: ''If a preliminary 
hearing has not been commenced within fourteen (14) days as required under subsection (a) an d 
unless such hearing has been postponed as provided in subsection (d), the defendant shall be released 
on recognizance."  Subsection (D) did not apply.  AS SUCH THE STATE OF MISSISSIPPI WAS 
OBLIGATED TO RELEASE MOVANT ON HIS OWN RECOGNIZANCE BY THE 15TH 
DAY; THIS DID NOT HAPPEN

FORMAL NOTICE OF DEFENDANT'S INTENT TO APPEAL DE NOVO  
Comes now before the court Matthew Reardon, your Defendant, whom hereby notices this court of his 
intention to appeal all guilty verdicts entered on the above referenced cause numbers to the Lafayette County 
Circuit Court for De Novo trial WITH a Jury Demand requested .   Defendant would simultaneously raise the 
notion that he has filed a timely  motion under MRCP Rule 59 in both th e Circuit Court of Lafayette County as well 
as th e Justice Court of Lafayette County which made reference prior to closing of Defendant's notice of appeal,  and 
hereby files this as an official notice of appeal in order to ensure his formal notice of appeal is promptly 
presented before the court in good t iming in a manner that best suits the court. 
DEFENDANT  acknowledges receipt of an order dated and entered 12/8/2021 denying his motion filed under MRCP 
Rule 59 further specifying Rule 1 states that the scope of "These rules govern procedure in the circuit courts, 
chancery courts, and county courts in all suits of a civil nature..."
DEFENDANT  would state for the record in response that a state-court decision is contrary to federal law if the 
state court "Applies a rule that contradicts controlling Supreme Court authority or �if the state court confronts a set of 
facts that are materially indistinguishable from a decision� of the Supreme Court, but nevertheless arrives at a 
different result. Williams v. Taylor, 529 U.S. 362, 406 (2000).  Further, The Supreme Court has consistently 
held that a conviction obtained by the knowing use of perjured testimony is fundamentally unfair, and must be set 
aside if there is any reasonable likelihood that the false testimony could have affected the judgment of the jury.� 
United States v. Agurs, 427 U.S. 97, 103 (1976)  
DEFENDANT  respectfully request s reconsideration for DISMISSAL OF CHARGES  based on all reasons listed out 
in his filed motion and the above cited controlling case law prior to a De Novo Appeal  Commencing in Circuit Court

This matter comes before the panel of Wilson, P.J., Greenlee and Emfinger, JJ., on 
the State�s motion for additional time to file its brief, which was due no later than

SUPPLEMENTAL STATEMENT OF ISSUES 
1) Did Appellant receive inadequate legal representation from his legal counsel 
which was prejudicial to Appellant and that adequate representation likely would 
have resulted in a different, favorable outcome to the Appellant? 
2) Should the Trial Judge have had subject matter jurisdiction to rule upon and deny 
an evidentiary hearing requested after: 
a) This trial judge came in receipt of emails originally sent to his court 
administrator1 by appellant detailing out significant grievances and 
sustained injury through what appellant described equated to a complete 
fundamental miscarriage of Justice through bizarre and shocking events 
which deprived appellant of key procedural and substantive due process 
rights. Appellant only did what he was advised to do by Ms Sturdivant 
through sending her a narrative of the shocking criminal acts and 
occurrences involving Lafayette County Officials which had fully suppressed� 
and got rid of appellant through some of the most unscrupulous of ways. 
Appellant stated his goal was to be granted an evidentiary hearing to give 
further evidence of the claims he stated in order to reverse his plea given 
under duress and through coercion which should have never been allowed to 
be considered to be valid. Appellant had no legal representation and a very 
limited legal knowledge at that time and simply reached out for advice from 
the chambers of the original trial judge. Did this create a conflict of 
interest in which the trial judge should have been obligated to 
recuse? 
1 The emails referenced to are attached as an exhibit to Appellant's motion to Recuse filed in Post�
Conviction Relief (L20-316). I do not share access to the electronic filing system to notate the proper 
number of the Clerk Paper (CP) to reference, so each reference will be made similar to this.

b) ' When adding in a mitigating factor that the initial assigned circuit court 
. judge for the post-conviction relief proceeding was Judge Kent Smith whom

had surrounded a high preponderancy of there being a conflict of 
interest?

ma'.ndatory obligation to safeguard the liberties of all citizens through

STATEMENT OF THE CASE 
Based U:pon a report created on May 30, 201 7, it was stated that on May 24, 
I 
2017 Jarrett Bundren, an investigator with Lafayette County Sheriff's 
I 
Department received via a phone call stating that I, Matthew Reardon, was making 
threats towards an individual by the name of Todd Lynch. Affiant has effortlessly made the claim 
that this entire fiasco amounted to a crime alleged that was never truly committed in order to suppress 
and prevent an outspoken conservative, 2nd amendment activist from speaking at a public forum 
which he, was slotted in advance to speak at on June 6, 2017; the day that just so happened to be 
election day for Robyn Tannehill. Completely violating the constitutional rights of another by way of 
a knowi~gly false charge which led to false imprisonment was not enough for Lafayette County 
Officials,; as they deprived nearly all procedural due process rights which all accused are to be 
afforded in order to contrive a conviction stemming from intimidation and fear, not facts and 
evidence'. Affiant was held illegally confined in the Lafayette County Detention Center from the time 
I 
of his arrbst, May 26 2017 until he received an initial/bond hearing on May 30 2017. This amounted 
to nearly 96 hours before movant was brought before a judge, twice the maximum length allowable 
under the Mississippi Rules of Criminal Procedure which had recently been updated prior to affiant' s 
arrest and incarceration to follow. 
MRCP Rule 5.l(b)(3) states if a person is taken into custody, the person shall be taken without 
unnecessary delay, and in no event later than forty-eight (48) hours after arrest, before a judge 
who shall proceed with an initial appearance. 
6 
\n--- Page 8 ---
a) Ifithe person arrested is not taken before a judge within forty-eight (48) hours, the person 
ddtained shall be released on execution of an appearance bond in the minimum amount set 
pursuant to Rule 8 and directed to appear at a specified time and place. 
b) Affiant tried to assert his complete innocence through a bill of sale proving not only that it 
was an impossibility for him to have committed the crime charged, but that in fact no crime 
was committed. 
c) Judge Carolyn Bell cut Affiant off from speaking on two separate occasions to stating he had 
"~he right to remain silent" just prior to setting a completely outrageous appearance bond. 
d) J1~dge Bell never cared to advise me that I had the right to a Preliminary Hearing. MRCrP 8.1 
li~ts a table recently updated approximately a month prior showing ranges for types of crimes.

STANDARD OF REVIEW 
An Individual moving for Post-Conviction Relief bears the burden of showing 
by a pr,eponderance of evidence that he is entitled to relief. Smith v. State, 129 So. 3d 
243, 245 (Miss. Ct. App. 2013). On review from the denial of a PCR motion, this Court 
"review[s] the trial court's findings of fact for clear error and its determinations oflaw 
I 
de novo." When presented with clear procedural and substantive due process rights 
3 Exhibit attached to back of this reply brief which goes to showing what appellant alleges was the 
fraudulent altering/reassignment of assigned judge on the matter 
11 
\n--- Page 13 ---
violations such as the violations which have been brought forth to the trial court and 
this appellate court, the state has a necessary obligation to offer corrective process 
ARGUMENT 
When ass~ssing all facts associated with the original criminal charge this post-conviction relief 
stems from, it should become clear to any examiner or onlooker that Procedural and Substantive 
Due Process Rights were deprived in some of the most egregious of ways. A State can never be 
allowed to procure a conviction stemming from complete deprivation of procedural due process 
rights of the accused in the weeks and months leading up to a plea being made. 
"The prohibition in the Constitution that no person shall be deprived of his 
I 
liberty rxcept by due process of law was intended to guarantee the protection 
of fund.amental and constitutional rights so that a fair trial may result, and 
where fundamental and constitutional rights are ignored due process does 
not exdt and a fair trial can not be had." Brooks v. State, 209 Miss. 150, 151 
(Miss. 1/950). It is the sole opinion of the appellant that this particular ruling gives 
reason to why Appellant submitted a motion for peremptory reversal and maintains 
the position that the matter upon which he submitted his peremptory reversal is 
indeed warranting of reversal and remand by this court based upon the deprivation of 
fundamental rights stated throughout this proceeding. 
The allowance of such while forgiving, forgetting, and overlooking of the manifest errors 
committed by completely depriving a presumably innocent citizen of his or her liberty 
while n~glecting obligations to provide the same rights to an accused that it is mandated 
! 
to provide to all others results in prejudice to the accused and has a high preponderance 
of leading to a Fundamental Miscarriage of Justice such as what had occurred here. 
Prejudice is actual harm resulting from the alleged constitutional violation. Thomas v. 
12 
\n--- Page 14 ---
Lewis,945 F.2d 1119, 1123 (9th Cir. 1991). In the extraordinary case, "[a] 'fundamental 
miscarriflge of justice' occurs when 'a constitutional violation has probably resulted in 
the conviction of one who is actually innocent."' Boyd v. Thompson,147 F.3d 1124, 1127 
(9th Cir.;1998) (quoting Murray,477 U.S. at 495-496, 106 S.Ct. 2639). "The requirement, 
in safeguarding the liberty of the citizen against deprivation through the action of the 
state, embodies the fundamental conceptions of justice which lie at the base of our civil 
and political institutions." Hebert v. Louisiana, 272 U.S. 312, 316, 317, 47 S.Ct. 103. It 
is a requirement that cannot be deemed to be satisfied by mere notice and hearing if a 
state ha:s contrived a conviction through the pretense of a trial which in truth is but 
' 
used as 'a means of depriving a defendant of liberty through a deliberate deception of 
the court. Such a contrivance by a state to procure the conviction and imprisonment of 
a defendant is an inconsistent with the rudimentary demands of justice as is the 
obtaining of a like result by intimidation. 
In the State's Brief, the Appellee contends that the Appellant failed to meet his burden 
of sho~g that any of his state or federal rights were violated and that he had a right 
to post-conviction relief. Appellant refutes this statement wholeheartedly, and further 
I 
states tp.at while he may not have sufficiently detailed out in his petition all state and 
federal :violations to the Appellee's particular preference, he did in fact raise the fact 
that indeed his procedural and substantive due process rights were violated, and 
Appellant has not wavered in these claims. Appellant strengthened his claims as time 
progressed and he gained a greater knowledge and understanding of the laws and the 
13 
\n--- Page 15 ---
serious deprivations he sustained of guaranteed rights afforded to all accused, which 
I 
the lack-there-of completely eliminates all fairness and equality from proceedings, and 
in no instance can these deprivations pass constitutional muster. In addition to this fact, 
a basic re{usal by the State to offer these same mandated rights it affords to all accused 
in criminal proceedings, and it's refusal to offer mandatory corrective process when it 
becomes aware of such deprivations occurring, equates to additional Federal violations 
of appellants 14th Amendment Rights which protect all citizens from being subjected by 
the State, to the deprivation of life, liberty, or property, without due process oflaw; nor 
the den~ng to any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws. 
' 
Appellant raises the constitutionality of a plea given in the weeks or months following 
such prejudice and deprivation and respectfully requests this honorable court to give 
answer as to how this can pass the constitutional muster. The Appellee claims the 
I applicat~on of a state harmless-error rule is, of course, a state question where it involves 
I 
only errqrs of state procedure or state law. But the error from which 
these petitioners suffered was a denial of rights guaranteed against invasion by the 
Fifth a:ri.d Fourteenth Amendments, rights rooted in the Bill of Rights, offered and 
championed in the Congress by James Madison, who told the Congress that the 
I 
"indepe~dent" federal courts would be the "guardians of those rights." Chapman v. 
Califor~ia, 386 U.S. 18, 21 (1967). Further, it has long been established through a 
multitu,de of cited case law, the court maintains discretion to address such issues on 
appeal even if they were waived and never properly presented to the trial court. See, 
e.g., Tinnon V. Martin, 716 So.2d 604, 613-14 c,r,r 52, 57-58) (Miss.1998); Fordice V. 
14 
\n--- Page 16 ---
Bryan, 6,51 So.2d 998, 1001 (Miss.1995); State Hwy. Comm'n of Miss. v. McDonald's 
I 
Corp., 509 So.2d 856, 860, 863 (Miss.1987)

AFFIDAVIT/DECLARATION OF TRUTH  
To: JOSEPH B EAST , SHERIFF OF LAFAYETTE COUNTY   711 JACKSON AVE E, OXFORD, MS 38655  
I, MATTHEW OLIVER REARDON  the undersigned, make this Affidavit/Declaration of Truth 
of my own free will, and I hereby affirm, declare and swear, under my oath and under the pains and 
penalties of perjury under the laws of the United States of America and of this state, that I am o f legal 
age and hereby attest that the statements, averments and information contained in this 
Affidavit/Declaration are true and correct to the best of my knowledge.  
This Affidavit/Declaration of Truth is lawful notification you, and is hereby made and se nt to you 
pursuant to the Federal  Constitution, specifically  the Bill of Rights, in particular, Amendments I, IV, V, 
VI, VII, IX , X, XIV;  and The Bill of Rights  detailed in Article 3  of the Mississippi  Constitution  
specifically sections 11, 12, 13, 14, 17,  23, 24, 25 , 26, and 26 -A.  This Affidavit/Declaration of Truth  
requires your written rebuttal to me, in kind, specific to each and every point of the subject matter stated 
herein, within 15 days, via your own sworn and notarized affidavit, using true fact , valid law and evidence 
support ing your rebuttal of the specific subject matter stated in this Affidavit/Declaration. You are hereby 
noticed that your failure to respond, as stipulated, and rebut, with particularity and specificity, anything 
with which you disagree in this Affidavit/Declar ation, is your lawful, legal and binding tacit agreement 
with and admission to the fact that everything in this Affidavit/Declaration is true, correct, legal, lawful, 
and fully binding upon you in any court in America, without your protest or objection and  that of those 
who represent you.  
1. Acts committed by you, JOSEPH EAST , acting as  SHERIFF OF LAFAYETTE COUNTY, MS
either supports and upholds the Constitutions, national and state, or oppose  and violates them.
2. You have taken an oath to support and uphold th e national and state Constitutions and are
constitutionally mandated to abide by that oath in the performance of your official duties.

\n--- Page 2 ---
3. You have no constitutional authority, or any other form of valid, lawful authority, to oppose and
violate the very docum ents to which you swore or affirmed your oath and under which you were
delegated by the people the limited authority to conduct the duties of your office.
4. The above three positions are true, factual, lawful and constitutionally ordained.
5. Through this Affidavit/Declaration of Truth, you are hereb y noticed of your alleged violations of
the below mentioned sections of the Mississippi Constitution  along with supporting facts to
corroborate the claims that are being made
You, Joseph B East, act ing as Sheriff, swore an oath to uphold and support the Constitution of the United 
States of America and the Your State Constitution, and pursuant to your oath, you are required to abide 
by that oath in the performance of your official duties. You have no Constitutional or other valid authority 
to defy the Constitutions, to which you owe your limited authority, delegated to you by and through the 
People, and to which you swore your oath; yet, by your actions against the People you have violated those 
oaths and engaged in myriad instances of incompetence, negligence, dereliction of duty, malfeasance, 
sedition, insurrection, treason and criminal, unconstitutional behavior rendering you unfit to hold public 
office.  
However, despite the above -stated factual, law ful positions, your unconstitutional actions, as described 
throughout this Affidavit/Declaration of Truth, clearly demonstrate how you have violated all of the above 
lawful positions, the Constitutions, your oath of office, acted against the public good by  knowingly and 
willfully violating the public trust and committing sedition and insurrection. Pursuant to your unlawful 
and unconstitutional actions, you have invoked the self -executing Sections 3 & 4 of the 14th Amendment 
to the National Constitution, the reby have lawfully vacated your office and forfeited all benefits thereof, 
including salary and pension. Please note that, as stated above and below, if you fail to specifically rebut, 
in kind, any of the charges, claims and positions set forth in this Aff idavit/Declaration, then, you tacitly 
\n--- Page 3 ---
admit to them, and these admissions will be lawfully used against you. The following paragraphs and 
others throughout this Affidavit/Declaration describe some of your unlawful, unconstitutional actions, 
which have harm ed myself and others:  
Based upon a report created on May 30, 2017, it was stated that on May 24, 2017 Jarrett 
Bundren, an investigator with Lafayette County Sheriff �s Department received via a phone 
call stating that I, Matthew Reardon, was making threats towards an individual by the name 
of Todd Lynch.  Affiant has effortlessly made the claim that this entire fiasco amounted 
to a crime alleged that was never truly co mmitted in order to suppress  and prevent an 
outspoken conservative, 2nd amendment activist  from speaking at a public forum which 
he was slotted in advance to speak at  on June 6, 2017 ; the day that just so happened to 
be election day  for Robyn Tannehill.  Completely violating the constitutional rights of 
another by way of a knowingly false charge which led to false imprisonment was not 
enough for Lafayette County Officials, as they deprived nearly all procedural due 
process rights which all accused are to be afforded in ord er to contrive a conviction 
stemming from intimidation and fear, not facts and evidence.  Affiant was held illegally 
confined in the Lafayette County Detention Center from the time of his arrest, May 26 2017 until he 
received an initial/bond hearing on May 30 2017.  This amounted to nearly 96 hours before movant 
was brought before a judge, twice the maximum length allowable under the Mississippi Rules of 
Criminal Procedure which had recently been updated prior to affiant�s arrest and incarceration to 
follow.   
MRCP Rule 5.1(b)(3) states if a person is taken into custody, the person shall be taken 
without unnecessary delay, and in no event later than forty -eight (48) hours after arrest, 
before a judge who shall proceed with an initia l appearance.  
\n--- Page 4 ---
a)If the person arrested is not taken before a judge within forty -eight (48) hours, the person
detained shall be released on execution of an appearance bond in the minimum amount set
pursuant to Rule 8 and directed to appear at a specified tim e and place.
b)Affiant tried to assert his complete innocence through a bill of sale proving not only that it was
an impossibility for him to have committed the crime charged, but that in fact no crime was
committed. Judge Carolyn Bell cut Affiant off from speaking on two separate occasions to
stating he had  �the right to remain silent� just prior to setting a completely outrageous
appearance bond.
c)Judge Bell never cared to advise me that I had the right to a Preliminary Hearing. MRCrP 8.1
lists a table rece ntly updated approximately a month prior showing ranges for types of crimes.
d)A crime receiving a maximum incarceration of 10 -years should receive a $5,000 -$50,000
Appearance Bond.  Affiant�s alleged crime received a maximum 5 -years incarceration yet was
set at $150,000. Due to it already going well beyond 48 -Hours before affiant was afforded
an initial hearing, Judge Bell was obligated to issuing an appearance bond of $5,000 or
releasing affiant on his personal recognizance.
e)Affiant makes claims of impr oper influence being the key factor in what had occurred and that
this move had likely  been  decided upon just prior to the initial hearing commencing
Affiant reasserts his claims of needing to get his daughter out of an ill -brought, fraudulent, and 
deceptive chancery court matter which just so happened to be coincidentally piled on at the same 
time to further stack the deck.  Upon pleading out following extended mental torture, Lafayette 
County Officials insisted on a banishment being a stipulation i n order to get rid of their 
problem and source of liability for at least the newly elected  mayor�s first term,  along 
\n--- Page 5 ---
with fully  disarming him, and insisting upon the  sign ing of  a covenant to not sue 
Lafayette County  and Oxford  employees and officials , Sher iff�s Department, and The 
Lynch�s.  However, exculpatory evidence  of innocence  existed all along , and that  a 
preliminary hearing was never afforded . Bond was set disproportionately and 
unconstitutionally high  in a clear violation of the 8th Amendment to the Federal 
Constitution  as the bond set at $150,000  was three times higher than the established 
guidelines  stated even for an alleged crime that could see twice the potential length of 
incarceration  if found guilty . A motion for Habeas Corpu s seeking bail reduction was 
filed June 5, 2017 alleging  that Affiant was  illegally confined and restrained of liberty 
in the Lafayette County  Detention Center  with bail in the amount of  $150,000 and 
that confinement was illegal because bail was excessive,  oppressive and beyond the  
financial means of Affiant or his  family and loved ones . This Habeas Corpus  Petition 
placed Lafayette County on notice of  violation s of the Eighth and Fourteenth 
Amendments to the United Stat es Constitution and  �� 26 and 29 of th e Mississippi 
Constitution  at that time.  At the  bare  minimum, this motion  commanded that the 
Sheriff produce the affiant and show good cause why he should not be discharged from 
such illegal confinement immediately or have his  bond  reduced to a constitutional amount.  
The Habeas Corpus was approved to proceed  in forma pauperis on June 7, 2017 . 
However, the approved petition was never proceeded upon and coincidentally Affiant 
was never even informed that this petition was filed on his behalf muc h less approved. 
Approximately 2 months after plea was entered,  A Psychologist in Tupelo, whom was 
the referring doctor for Lafayette County Chancery Court, notated coercion on his 
report as the chief reason for such plea.  In the following months and year s upon the 
discovery of additional facts and evidence, Lafayette County primarily through its 
most  Sheriff Joseph East  has refused to provide  equal protections under  its laws

and stands accused of continued intentional depriv ation of  the Affiant�s civil rights in

Officials  in Direct  violatio n of State and Federal Law, making his plea involuntary and void

That on July 7, 2020 Affiant filed his post-conviction  relief in Lafayette County Circuit Court

Between July, 2021 and August, 2021, I, Matthew Oliver Reardon, had obtained press credentials , 
further providing official notice that I was a recognized and credentialed 
Reporter/Photographer /Journalist  as a member in  good standing with the Constitution First 
Amendment Press Association for independent work in journalism and reporting. It was around

yields the fundamental right to petition the Government for Redress is and always has been 
inherently  vested in, and derived from the people.  All government of right originates with the
people, is founded u pon their will only, and is instituted solely for the good of the whole. 
Furthermore, the people of this state have the inherent, sole, and exclusive right to regulate the I 
\n--- Page 8 ---
internal government and police thereof. This is a guaranteed right of all citizens o f the state under 
Article 3 Section 6, yet another basic right requiring no credentials

Section 802 of the USA PATRIOT Act (Pub. L. No. 107 -52) Expanded the definition of terrorism to cover 
�"domestic,"" as opposed to international, terrorism. A person engages in domestic terrorism if they do an 
act "dangerous to human life" that is a violation of the criminal laws of a state or the United States , if the 
act appears to be intended to: (i) intimidate or coerce a civilian population; (ii) influence the policy of a 
government by intimidation or coercion;  
 
Lastly, current policing, fining, arrests and harassment throughout Lafayette County , MS  is in violation 
of not only First Amendment �abridging the right of people to peaceably assemble� but more narrowly:   
Title 18 U.S.C., Section 242 - Deprivation of Rights Under Color of Law : Whoever, under color of any 
law, statute, ordinance, regulation, or custom, willfully subjects any person in any State, Territory, 
\n--- Page 11 ---
Commonwealth, Possession, or District to the deprivation of any rights, privileges, or immunities secured 
or protected by the Constitution or laws of the United States, or to different punis hments, pains, or 
penalties, on account of such person being an alien, or by reason of his color, or race, than are prescribed 
for the punishment of citizens, shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than one year, or both; 
and if bodily inju ry results from the acts committed in violation of this section or if such acts include the 
use, attempted use, or threatened use of a dangerous weapon, explosives, or fire, shall be fined under this 
title or imprisoned not more than ten years, or both; an d if death results from the acts committed in 
violation of this section or if such acts include kidnapping or an attempt to kidnap, aggravated sexual 
abuse, or an attempt to commit aggravated sexual abuse, or an attempt to kill, shall be fined under this 
title, or imprisoned for any term of years or for life, or both, or may be sentenced to death.  
Due to  your unconstitutional actions  and draconian measures  as described throughout this 
Affidavit/Declaration, you have invoked the referenced Sections 3 & 4 of  the 14th Amendment . As such 
you have  lawfully vacated your office  and forfeited all benefits thereof, including salary and pension.

averments made in this Affidavit/Declaration, then, you tacitly agree with and admit to them. Pursuant to

Truth, then rebut to me that with which you  disagree, with particularity, within (1 5) fifteen  days of receipt 
thereof, by means of your own written, notarized affidavit of truth, based on specific, true, relevant fact 
and valid law to support your disagreement, attesting to your rebuttal and suppor tive positions, as valid 
and lawful, under the pains and penalties of perjury under the laws of the United States of America and 
the State of Mississippi . An unrebutted affidavit stands as truth and fact before any court. Your failure to 
respond, as stipul ated, is your tacit agreement with and admission to the fact that everything in this 
Affidavit/Declaration of Truth is true, correct, legal, lawful, and is your irrevocable admission attesting to 
\n--- Page 12 ---
this, fully binding upon you in any court of law in America,  without your protest, objection and that of

APPELLANT'S HARSH OBJECTION AND REBUKE OF APPELLEES MOTION FOR 
ENLARGEMENT OF TIME WITHIN WHICH TO FILE APPELLEE'S BRIEF 
COMES NOW YOUR APPELLANT, MATTHEW OLIVER REARDON, WHOM 
STRONGLY OBJECTS, REBUKES, AND CONDEMNS THE APPELLEE'S MOTION FOR 
ENLARGEMENT OF TIME WITHIN WHICH TO FILE APPELLEE'S BRIEF AND IN 
BRINGING THIS MOTION CONTAINING SUPPORTING FACTS STATES HIS 
REASONS WHY. 
1) APPELLANT ASSERTS THAT THIS REQUEST AND IN PARTICULAR THE WAY 
APPELLEE CHOSE TO BRING SUCH REQUEST IS NOT ONLY HIGHLY 
DISTASTEFUL, BUT HAS WITHOUT DOUBT TAKEN IT''S PLACE AS THE MOST 
OBNOXIOUS, DISRESPECTFUL REQUEST APPELLANT HAS EVER OBSERVED 
2) THE APPELLEE WAS FULLY AW ARE OF A DEADLINE BEING DECEMBER 3RD TO 
HA VE HER APPELLEE BRIEF SUBMITTED YET FAILED TO EVEN SUBMIT HER 
MOTION FOR TIME EXTENSION TIMELY AND EVEN MISSED THE DEADLINE 
BY 4-DAYS TIME. NOT ONLY WAS HER BRIEF NOT SUBMITTED TIMELY, BUT THE 
APPELLEE FAILED TO EVEN GET AN ELECTRONICALLY FILED MOTION FOR TIME 
EXTENTION SUBMITTED BEFORE THIS COURT UNTIL 4-DAYS AFTER HER 
APPELLEE'S BRIEF WAS DUE. THIS IS SIMPLY UNACCEPTABLE, PARTICULARLY 
. WHEN APPELLANT'S RESPONSE WAS STRICKEN FROM CONSIDERATION BY THIS 
COURT AFTER HE ELECTRONICALLY FAXED HIS REBUTTAL TO APPELLEE'S 
RESPONSE INTO THIS COURT DUE TO REDUCED RESOURCES AND TIME. 
\n--- Page 2 ---
3) APPELLEES BRIEF SHOULD NOT BE CONSIDERED DUE TO THE FACT THAT NOT 
ONLY WAS APPELLEES LONG AWAITED BRIEF NOT SUBMITTED BY 12/3/2021, 
BUT NO MOTION WHATSOEVER WAS FILED BY THAT DATE. APPELLANT'S PRO�
SE REBUTTAL WAS STRUCK FROM.CONSIDERATION BY THIS COURT OVER 
IT BEING AN ELECTRONICALLY FAXED REBUTTAL. APPELLANT ARGUES IN ALL 
FAIRNESS AND CONSIDERATION THAT APPELLEE DOES THIS FOR A LIVING, 
THAT HA YING OTHER BRIEF'S TO FILE IS A COMPLETELY DISRESPECTFUL 
RESPONSE TO APPELLANT AND THIS COURT, AND THAT APPELLEE SHOULD BE 
BARRED FROM RECEIVING SPECIAL FAVORS OR CONSIDERATIONS THROUGH 
HER UNTIMELY REQUEST FOR TIME EXTENSION SUBMITTED. 
4) THAT IF APPELLEE IS IN FACT PERMITTED TO SUBMIT HER APPELLEES BRIEF 
THAT APPELLANT FOR GREAT CAUSE AND REASON MOVES THIS COURT FOR 
ORAL ARGUMENTS ON THE BASIS OF RAISED AND FURTHER IGNORED DUE 
PROCESS/CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS VIOLATIONS ALLEGED WITHOUT 
MANDATED CORRECTIVE PROCESS EVER BEING ASSESSED, OFFERED, OR 
INITIATED BY THE STATE OF MISSISSIPPI AND IN PARTICULAR THE OFFICE OF 
THE APPELLEE.

1 
 STATEMEN T OF THE ISSUE  
 
May a party seeking post -conviction relief  prevail  when his 
motion fails to substantially show the denial of a state or federal 
right?  
STATEMENT OF THE CASE  
 
Appellant Matthew Reardon waived his right to an indictment and 
entered a petition to plead guilty by  a bill of information to the crime of 
aggravated stalking. (CP 101 , 103, 105). The circuit court accepted 
Reardon�s  guilty plea. (CP 160). In accord with his plea bargain , Reardon 
was sentenced to serve five years in the custody of the Mississippi 
Departm ent of Corrections with credit for time served, and the rest of his 
sentence was suspended to be served on post-release supervision  (PRS). 
(CP 119 �20). As  a special condition of his PRS, Reardon was banished 
from Lafayette County. (CP 120; 163).  
About thre e years later , Reardon moved for  post-conviction relief 
(PCR), asserting without argument or sufficient details that the State violated his First, Eighth, and Fourteenth Amendment constitutional 
rights when  he entered his guilty plea and that he had involu ntarily 
entered his plea based on  a denial of his right to due process. (CP 8 �9). 
The circuit court denied his PCR motion,  finding that it plainly appeared 
\n--- Page 6 ---
2 
 from the face of the motion and prior proceedings that Reardon had no 
right  to relief and that his c laims were contradicted by the transcript of 
the plea and sentencing hearing s. (CP 18).  
After the Order Denying Relief was entered, Reardon filed a Motion 
for Rehearing, a Motion for Recusal, and a Motion to Compel Evidence. 
(CP 19 �20; 21; 27 �28). All were denied. (CP 33). Reardon timely appeals. 
(CP 36).  
STANDARD OF REVIEW  
 A PCR movant bears the burden of showing by a preponderance of 
the evidence that he is entitled to relief. Smith v. State , 129 So. 3d 243, 
245 (Miss. Ct. App. 2013). On review from the denial of a PCR motion, 
this Court �review[s] the trial court�s findings of fact for clear error and 
its determinations of law de novo.� Id. The trial court�s denial of a PCR 
motion will be affirmed in every case in which  the movant fails to 
substantially show that he is entitled to relief based on  the denial of a 
state or fe deral right. Id. 
SUMMARY OF THE ARGUMENT 
 The trial court did not err by denying Reardon�s PCR motion. 
Reardon failed to meet his burden of showing that any of his state or 
\n--- Page 7 ---
3 
 federal rights were violated and that he had a right  to post-conviction 
relief. The arguments that he raises for the first time on appeal are 
barred and should not be considered by this Court.  
ARGUMENT 
For the first time on appeal , Reardon argues that (1) his attorney 
rendered �malfeasant legal representation; (2) he was coerced  to pl ead 
guilty through the imposition of an excessive bond; ( 3) he was denied due 
process  due to a delayed initial appearance and the denial of his right to 
a preliminary hearing; and (4) he has exculpatory  evidence that entitles 
him to relief. (Appellant�s Br ief 6�15).1 These are markedly different from 
the arguments he presented in the circuit court. There, he presented 
(with no argument ) claims that the State violated his First, Eighth, and 
Fourteenth Amendment constitutional rights when  he entered his guilty 
plea, and that he had involuntarily entered his plea based on  a denial of 
his right to due process.2 (CP 8 �9).  
 
1 Reardon�s fifteen -page brief contains no page numbers. So the State will cite  
the actual page number of the document.  
 
2 After the trial court ruled on his PCR motion, Reardon filed a motion for 
rehearing and a motion for recusal. In his statement of the issues, Reardon lists the 
denial of the motion for recusal as an issue to be a rgued. (Appellant�s Brief 5). But  
that issue was not briefed and should not be addressed by this Court.  See Corrothers 
\n--- Page 8 ---
4 
 In his PCR motion, Reardon presented issues but included no 
argument in support of his claims. He does now, but, t o the extent that 
Reardon tries to relitigate his originally-presented claims  in an untimely 
attempt to meet his burden under the PCR Act , this Court should find 
that the new arguments are barred. See Smith , 129 So. 3d at 246 (noting 
that arguments not presented to the circuit court are procedurally barred 
from appellate review). Even so, Reardon�s arguments are still 
insufficient to demonstrate that he is entitled to post-conviction relief.  
As for the claims he presented to the circuit court , it correc tly 
concluded that Reardon�s PCR claims were unsupported and were 
contradicted by the record. (CP 18). The record shows that Reardon 
voluntarily pleaded guilty. (CP 158). He told the trial court that he was 
satisfied with his counsel�s performance and that  the decision to plead 
guilty was his own ; he was not coerced. (CP 152; 158). And he admitted 
that he committed the crime for which he was charged. (CP 158). The 
issues rejected by the circuit court lack merit.  
 
v. State , 148 So. 3d 278, 323 (Miss. 2014) ; Smith v. State , 118 So. 3d 180, 182 (Miss. 
Ct. App. 2013) . 
\n--- Page 9 ---
5 
 The issues he presents for the first time on appeal are barred and 
are also without merit.  Reardon does not raise a claim of ineffective 
assistance of counsel. Instead, he argues that his attorney provided 
malfeasant legal representation when he coerced him to plead guilty by 
warning him that , if he did not, the State would �pile on� additional 
charges. (Appellant�s Brief 13). Reardon�s claim that he was coerced 
contradicts his testimony at his plea proceeding that he was not. (CP 
158). Further, the offering of the complained -of advice was not coerciv e 
and did not amount to ineffective assistance of counsel.  
This Court has held that counsel has a duty to fairly advise his 
client about the likely outcome of his case. Middlebrook v. State , 964 So. 
2d 638, 640 (Miss. Ct. App. 2007). Further , �[i]f [an] attorney believes 
that it is in his client�s best interest to plead guilty, it is his duty to inform 
him of that fact.� Id. Here, counsel�s suggestion that the State planned to 
bring more charges against Reardon was not improper because it was true. (CP 161 �63). This advice does not warrant any relief.  
Neither do his claims regarding the delay or denial of his initial 
appearance or preliminary hearing , his claim that he was entitled to pre-
plea bond relief,  and his claim that he possesses exculpator y evidence. A  
\n--- Page 10 ---
6 
 valid guilty plea waives pretrial bond issues. McComb v. State , 135 So. 
3d 928, 932 (Miss. Ct. App. 2014). It also waives the right to an initial 
appearance and a preliminary hearing , Reeves v. State , 256 So. 3d 632, 
635 (Miss. Ct. App. 2018 ), and  it waives the right to present a defense 
including exculpatory evidence and to require the State to prove the 
elements of the offense beyond a reasonable doubt , Worth v. State , 223 
So. 3d 844, 852 (Miss. Ct. App. 2017).  So these arguments lack m erit. 
CONCLUSION 
The issues presented by Reardon are barred and lack merit. For 
these reasons, the State of Mississippi requests that this Honorable 
Court affirm the denial of Reardon�s PCR motion.

Rule 3 (c) of the Mississippi Rules of Civil Procedure,  adopted by the Mississippi Supreme Court, 
states:
Proceeding In Forma Pauperis. A party may proceed in  forma pauperis in accordance with 
sections 11-53-17 and 11-53-19 of the Mississippi Code Annotated.
The court may, however, on the motion of any party, on the  motion of the clerk of the court, or 
on its own initiative,  examine the affiant as to the facts and circumstances of his  pauperism.
State Statutes:
Title 11. Civil Practice and Procedure 
Chapter 53. Costs
Miss. Code Ann. � 11-53-17 
Poor persons may sue without security for costs. A  citizen may commence any civil 
action, or answer a  rule for security for costs in any court without  being required to 
prepay fees or give security for  costs, before or after commencing suit, by taking 
and subscribing via affidavit.

I, Matthew Oliver Reardon, do solemnly s,vear that I am a citizen of the State of Mississippi, and that

Further, it is respectfully requested that this matter receive preferentia l docketing and an expedited niling 
due to matters involving tl1e care of a minor child, the serious grievances sustained, and the irreparab le 
damages already incurred due to the handling of this matter by the comt appealed from. 
As an added addendum to this motion to proceed in fonna pauperis, Appellant brings the following four 
stated grievances as additional meritorious issues needing proper address by this court and raises these 
questions to this court in seeking proper redress. 
1)Does a trial judge whose order is appealed maintain authority to rule on and deny an appellant's In
Fonua Pauperis Affidavit to proceed without prepayment of security costs?
2)Would a denial of such IFP Affidavit by the trial judge appealed from constitute impropriety on
behalf of the judge by 
subjecting the appellant to unfair, prejudicial conditions fhrther depriving
that individual of equal protections under the mies of the court and the laws of the state?
2022

3)Should a notice of appeal and petition for writ of mandamus filed against the trial judge halt a11
fmther actions and rulings from the trial judge until such time the appeal and/ or writ of mandamus
are ruled upon by the appellate court?
4)Does a trial Judge have authority to simply deny a sworn IFP Affidavit without holding a
determination hearing regarding the individual's indigent status and inability to pay costs?
Rule 3 ( c) of the Mississippi Rules of Civil Procedure, adopted by the Mississippi Supreme Court, states: 
A party may proceed in fonna pauperis in accordance with sections 11-53-17 and 11-53-19 of the 
Mississippi Code Annotated. 
Section 11-53-17 of the MS Code Annotated states:  
Poor persons may sue without security for costs. A citizen may commence any civil action, or answer a 
rule for security for costs in any court without being required to prepay fees or give security for costs, 
before or after commencing suit, by taking-and subscribing via (the above stated) affidavit. 
The above stated section states a citizen may commence any civil action in any court without being required 
to prepay fees before or after co1mnencing 
suit. Appellant reads and interprets that language precisely the 
way it is written. however according to the staff attorney for the trial judge appealed from, the ability to 
proceed in fonna pauperis is solely reserved for parental termination cases. This is a matter raising 
significant concern as it clearly appears t
o be a deviance from established, written procedure, and one that 
gives off the impression of it being a rogue and possibly isolated event and one that is an unfair, 
potentially unethical call made. As such, appellant raises his grievances to this high court for its further 
in
terpretatio n on the aggrieved matters at hand due to numerous occasions in the past couple of years, 
appellant has raised issues surrounding the constitutionality of demands, decisions, and rulings made by the 
Chancery Court of Lafayette County and in particular the trial judge appealed from.

IN THE THIRD CIRCUIT COURT FOR LAFAYETTE COUNTY MISSISSIPPI 
IAFAYITTE COUNTI 
STATE OF MISSISSIPPI -Fl LED

MATT REARDON MAR 2 8 2022 
JEFF BUSBY Cause No. LKl 7-295 
CIR(JJ_IT CLERK ----------- ~ 1~p ~--------------
Amended Motion for Relief from Judgment or Order 
Comes now before the court Matthew Reardon, the movant, and respectfully moves this 
court for relief pursuant to Rule 60 of The Mississippi Rules of Civil Procedure. Movant 
acknowledges that he has a currently filed appeal in the MS Supreme Court which addresses the 
Post-Conviction Relief filed in this court on July 7, 2020. However, the original criminal cause 
was not properly addressed in matters movant now feels were the best of ways, because in fact 
pursuant to the Mississippi Rules of Civil Procedure which is the trne governing doctrine on 
movant's Post Conviction Relief, the Judgement he filed his post-conviction relief upon should 
have been rendered void and invalid, which in turn burdened the movant in perfecting his appeal 
of the denied post-conviction relief Movant has long alleged this complaint was FRAUDULENT, 
without justification, merit, and evidence since day one and movant properly raises the 
constitutionality of the original criminal cause LKl 7-295 under THE EXCLUSIONARY RULE 
and now asks that this court properly declare the plea to be void as Frnit of the Poisonous Tree 
based upon well settled and established law and principal surrounding the deprivation of key 
procedural and substantive due process rights. Movant states his motion will show that the State 
of Mississippi has had an obligation and has known of its obligation to ensure the accused are 
afforded Due Process in order to safeguard the constitutional rights of its citizens and to preserve 
equality and integrity. The State of Mississippi is well aware of the consequences derived from 
depriving a citizen of his/her due process rights, including the requirement in and that in the event 
\n--- Page 2 ---
2 it is made aware of constitutional/due process right violations  that it is mandated, and further has 
an absolute obligation to provide corrective process pursuant to not only its own stated guidelines, 
rules, and laws but well- established Federal Case Law as well.  
MOVANT HEREBY MAKES HIS ARGUMENT AND OFFERS IN SUPPORT 
I. The movant in this cause brings this motion asserting the following meritorious reasons this
trial court should grant relief under Rule 60: (1)  fraud, misrepresentation, or other
misconduct of an adverse party, (3) newly discovered evidence which by due diligence could
not have been discovered in time to move for a new trial under Rule 59(b), (4) the judgment
is void, and (6) any other reason justifying relief from the judgment.
II. A party may move to set aside a void judgment pursuant to MRCP Rule 60(b)(4) more tha n
six months after the entry of a judgment if the delay in moving for relief was reasonable. See
Ladner v. Logan, 857 So. 2d 764, 770 (Miss. 2003)
III. . A judgment is void if the trial court lacked jurisdiction over the subject matter or the parties
or acted  in a manner inconsistent with due process of law. See Bryant, Inc. v. Walters, 493
So. 2d 933, 938 (Miss. 1986). 
IV. The trial court has discretion to grant or deny a Rule 60(b) motion, unless the judgment is
void, in w
hich case the court is required to set aside the judgment. See Sartain v. White,
588 So. 2d 204, 211 (Miss. 1991) . The key word in this being the court is REQUIRED TO
SET ASIDE A VOID JUDGEMENT.
V. �This rule does not limit the power of a court to entertain an independent action to relieve a
party from a judgment, order, or proceeding, or to set aside a judgment for fraud upon the
court.�  Rule 60 - Relief from Judgment or Order , Miss. R. Civ. P. 60
\n--- Page 3 ---
3 
 VI. Currently an appeal sits before the Mississippi Court of Appe als in cause number L20-316 
based upon a denial by this court on movant �s Petition for Post- Conviction Relief he filed on 
July 7, 2020 seeking an evidentiary hearing and ultimately relief through reversal of this  
criminal cause. (PETITION, EXHIBITS, AND DENIAL ATTACHED AS EXHIBIT  A).  
VII. The first denial by this court (Exhibit A) came July 30, 2020 to which movant refiled a timely  
�Motion for Re -Hearing under MRCP Rule 59 Before the Honorable Judge Kent 
Smith � due to his post -conviction relief originally being assigned to the newly appointed 
Circuit Court Judge Kent Smith .  Th e trial court summarily denied  this Motion for Re -
Hearing  under MRCP Rule 59 which was filed  based upon newly discovered evidence and a 
plethora of violations aggrieved by movant due to the highly immoral and unconstitutional 
acts committed on behalf of the State  (MOTION AND DENIAL ATTACHED AS EXHIBIT 
B).   
 
VIII.  Up until August 12, 2020 the assigned Judge was newly appointed Circuit Judge Kent Smith, 
yet for reasons unbeknownst to movant , Judge John Kelly Luther continued ruling on and 
further denying each of movant �s motions and petitions . (EVIDENCE OR BEFORE AND 
AFTER ALTERATION OF JUDGE IS ATTACHED AS EXHIBIT C) 
 
IX. The Circuit Court Judge John Kelly Luther had upfront knowledge of fraud ulent acts and 
organized crime committed on behalf of  Officials in Lafayette County and on behalf of the 
State of Mississippi  which Movant claimed in  multiple instances rose to the level of 
Organized Crime and significant intentional violations of the RICO Act.

and it was an obligation that Lafayette County Sheriff�s Department had known upfront of

and Federal Law have yet to be arg ued in this matter, this unquestionably is the case.
\n--- Page 7 ---
7 XIX. MRCP Rule 5.1(b)(3) states if a person is taken into custody, the person shall be taken
without unnecessary delay, and in no event later than forty- eight (48) hours after arrest,
before a judge who sha ll proceed with an initial appearance.
XX. The cited  rule goes on to state  If the person arrested is not taken before a judge within forty-
eight (48) hours, the person detained shall be released on execution of an appearance bondin the minimum amount set pursuant to Rule 8 and directed to appear at a specified time andplace. Rule 5.1(b)(3) conforms to the United States Supreme Court's holdings in Gersteinv.Pugh  , 420 U.S. 103, 95 S. Ct. 854, 43 L. Ed. 2d 54
 (1975), and  County of Riverside
v.McLaughlin  , 500 U.S. 44, 111 S. Ct. 1661, 114 L. Ed. 2d 49  (1991).
XXI. Due Process rights entitle the defendant in a cause receive a preliminary hearing to determineif there is enough evidence warranting the defendant to stand trial. Movant was never advised
of his right to a preliminary hearing.  Due to it taking twice the allowable time to bringmovant before a judge for an initial appearance, Movant was entitled to his bail being set at
the minimum amount on the chart in Rule 8, which is  $5,000, NOT $150,000.  However,
Rule 6.1 states that if a preliminary hearing is not commenced within fourteen (14) days asrequired by subsection (a), and is not postponed as allowed by subsection (d), the defendantshall be released on recognizance, unless the offense is non- bailable or release is prohibited
by Article 3, Section 29(2) of the Mississippi Constitution of 1890. THEREFORE, Due to
movant never being afforded a preliminary hearing, nor  a preliminary hearing being set
within 14 days, The State had an obligation to Release on Recognizance, further directing
movant to appear at a specified time and place pursuant to  MRCP  Rule 5.1(b)(3).   This is not
an opinion and it is not a discretionary rule that The Prosecution can pick and choose when
to use, but rather  this is  a statement in fact and is evident by the wording in Rule 6.1 �The
Defendant SHALL be released on recognizance.
\n--- Page 8 ---

IN THE UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT  
FOR THE NORTHERN DISTRICT OF MISSISSIPPI  
OXFORD DIVISION  
 
MATTHEW OLIVER REARDON                   PLAINTIFF  
         
v.                                                 CIVIL ACTION NO.: 3:2 2-cv-50-SA-JMV  
     
STATE OF MISSISSIPPI, ET AL.             DEFENDANT S 
 
ORDE R 
 
Plaintiff Matthew Oliver Reardon  filed a complaint [1] against various people , entities , and 
the like  on April 6, 2022. By order [6] of this Court, plaintiff�s motion to proceed in forma pauperis  
was granted on April 8, 2022. However, pursuant to said order, service of process was withheld at 
that time. The Court finds that service of process shall  now be issued as to the named defendants. 
However, the re are various named defendants for whom no address is provided in the complaint 
[1]. 
Accordingly, it is ORDERED that the clerk shall issue process for the following defendants 
named in the complaint: Lafayette County Sheriff�s Department , Lafayette County  Chancery 
Court, Communicare, Dr. Sandy Rogers, Rachel Alcorn, and Susan Beard.  U.S. Marshal shall 
serve process upon these defendants in accordance with 28 U.S.C. � 1915.  
It is further ORDERED that plaintiff  shall have fourteen (14) days from the date of  this 
Order to provide an address  for the following defendants so that they may be served  with process : 
State of Mississippi, Lafayette County, Mississippi, Joseph B. East, David O�Donnell, Sherry 
Wall, and Jeff Busby. 
SO ORDERED AND ADJUDGED, this the  3rd day of  June , 2022.  
      /s/ Jane M. Virden  
       UNITED STATES MAGISTRATE JUDGE
